title: Deep Learning meets Nonparametric Regression: Are Weight-Decayed DNNs Locally Adaptive?

reviews.summary_of_the_paper:
This paper examines training a specific neural network (namely parallel neural net) using weight decay and shows that the error rate matches the minimax bound for a specific class of functions (i.e., functions with bounded variation). 

It looks like a quite interesting effort and the result also appears to be significant. But I worry the paper is not presented at the level for general audience in ICLR. For example, I have a few questions/confusions after reading the submission: 

1. What exactly is weight decay? I actually could not find its definition/elaboration except for eq. 2, which is just L2 regularization? I was guessing it could relate to learning rate, momentum, some dynamic shrinkage on the fly...
2. Do we assume that Eq.2 can be optimally solved? It is a minimax result related to sample complexity so I believe the authors assume that Eq. can be optimally solved --- I think sometimes ignoring computational tractability is fine (and perhaps had been a standard practice in statistics) but it would be helpful to bring this upfront. 
3. What's so special about parallel NN? I think most audience wants to understand at a higher level where the magic happens and why the magic wont happen for the standard MLP, e.g., only limit of existing theoretical tools or parallel NN possesses some special structure? 
----
Thank you for addressing the presentation concern. I have raised my scores to 6 -- do not feel am an expert in this area so no confidence to move to 7.
reviews.strength_and_weaknesses:
Strength: The authors can show that a specific kind of neural net achieves minimax rate for an interesting function family. Some of the techniques appear to be significant (e.g., hacking into covering numbers). 

Weakness: there is room for improvement in presentation.
reviews.clarity_quality_novelty_and_reproducibil: I can mostly follow the paper but was confused by some details from time to time.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: I think the substance is above the bar of ICLR; the revision addressed my presentation concern.

This paper examines training a specific neural network (namely parallel neural net) using weight decay and shows that the error rate matches the minimax bound for a specific class of functions (i.e., functions with bounded variation). 

It looks like a quite interesting effort and the result also appears to be significant. But I worry the paper is not presented at the level for general audience in ICLR. For example, I have a few questions/confusions after reading the submission: 

1. What exactly is weight decay? I actually could not find its definition/elaboration except for eq. 2, which is just L2 regularization? I was guessing it could relate to learning rate, momentum, some dynamic shrinkage on the fly...
2. Do we assume that Eq.2 can be optimally solved? It is a minimax result related to sample complexity so I believe the authors assume that Eq. can be optimally solved --- I think sometimes ignoring computational tractability is fine (and perhaps had been a standard practice in statistics) but it would be helpful to bring this upfront. 
3. What's so special about parallel NN? I think most audience wants to understand at a higher level where the magic happens and why the magic wont happen for the standard MLP, e.g., only limit of existing theoretical tools or parallel NN possesses some special structure?

Strength And Weaknesses:
Strength: The authors can show that a specific kind of neural net achieves minimax rate for an interesting function family. Some of the techniques appear to be significant (e.g., hacking into covering numbers). 

Weakness: there is room for improvement in presentation.

Clarity Quality Novelty And Reproducibility:
I can mostly follow the paper but was confused by some details from time to time.

Summary Of The Review:
I think the substance is above the bar of ICLR; the revision addressed my presentation concern.

reviews.text:
# Expanded Peer Review

## Summary of the Paper

This paper investigates the theoretical properties of deep neural networks through the lens of classical nonparametric regression, specifically examining whether weight-decayed deep ReLU networks can achieve local adaptivity—the ability to optimally estimate functions with heterogeneous smoothness. The authors focus on a specialized architecture called "parallel neural networks" (PNNs), which consist of an ensemble of subnetworks whose outputs are summed. Their main contribution is proving that standard ℓ2 regularization (weight decay) in training L-layer parallel ReLU networks is equivalent to promoting ℓp-sparsity (where p = 2/L) in the coefficient vector of learned function bases. Using this equivalence, they establish that parallel NNs can achieve estimation errors arbitrarily close to minimax rates for both Besov and Bounded Variation (BV) function classes, with the error rate improving exponentially as the network gets deeper. The paper provides a theoretical explanation for why depth matters and how neural networks can outperform kernel methods on certain function classes.

The work appears to be a significant theoretical contribution to understanding deep learning through the classical nonparametric regression perspective. However, there are notable presentation and clarity issues that limit its accessibility to the general ICLR audience.

## Strengths

**Strong theoretical contributions**: The paper makes several important theoretical advances. Most notably, the authors establish that parallel neural networks with standard ℓ2 regularization can achieve near-minimax rates for estimating functions in Besov and BV spaces without requiring architecture tuning—only regularization parameter tuning. This is a substantial improvement over prior work (Suzuki 2018, Parhi & Nowak 2021a;c) which either required non-standard activations, artificial sparsity constraints on weights, or careful architectural choices for each function class.

**Novel technical contributions**: The paper introduces several technically sophisticated tools that appear to be of independent interest. The equivalence result in Proposition 4 connecting ℓ2 regularization to ℓp-sparsity is elegant, though the authors acknowledge this builds on folklore from prior work (Savarese et al. 2019). More significantly, the covering number analysis in Theorem 5 provides bounds that do not depend on the number of subnetworks M, which is a non-trivial technical achievement. The decomposition strategy in Equation (4), which handles an unconstrained subspace with finite dimension while bounding the metric entropy of the constrained complement, represents careful technical work.

**Depth-dependent convergence rates**: The result that deeper networks achieve rates closer to minimax optimality (with the gap shrinking as p = 2/L → 0) provides theoretical insight into why empirically deeper networks often perform better. Corollary 2 shows that with L ≳ log n layers, the MSE approaches ˜O(n^{-2α/(2α+d)}(1-o(1))), which is nearly optimal up to logarithmic factors. This is a compelling theoretical explanation for a well-observed empirical phenomenon.

**Comprehensive treatment of function classes**: The paper successfully handles both Besov spaces (with parameters α, p, q) and Bounded Variation classes simultaneously, demonstrating adaptivity across different smoothness parameters. The embedding relationship between BV^(m) and Besov spaces (Equation 1) is leveraged effectively to extend results from Besov to BV classes.

**Experimental validation**: The numerical experiments in Section 5 and Appendix H demonstrate that parallel neural networks empirically achieve local adaptivity comparable to trend filtering and wavelet denoising, which are known to be minimax-optimal for BV classes. The visualization of active subnetworks (Figures 3c, 3f) provides intuitive evidence that ℓ2 regularization induces sparsity in the learned representation.

## Weaknesses

### Major Issues

**Lack of clarity on weight decay definition**: The paper uses "weight decay" and "ℓ2 regularization" interchangeably without clearly distinguishing between them. While Equation (2) presents the mathematical formulation as standard ℓ2 regularization (adding λ∑||W^(ℓ)_j||²_F to the loss), weight decay in practical deep learning often refers to multiplicative parameter shrinkage during optimization (as mentioned briefly in the introduction). The paper would benefit from explicitly clarifying this distinction early on and explaining why the analysis focuses on the regularized objective rather than the implicit regularization that occurs during stochastic gradient descent. The statement "we ignore the computation issue and focus on the global optimal solution to this problem" (page 4) acknowledges this gap but does not adequately address it for readers unfamiliar with the distinction between explicit and implicit regularization.

**Global optimality assumption**: The analysis assumes that the optimization problem in Equation (2) can be solved to global optimality. This is a strong assumption that deserves more prominent discussion. While the authors cite Choromanska et al. (2015) to justify this assumption, the cited work does not provide general guarantees for deep networks beyond specific cases. For a paper aimed at the ICLR audience, which includes practitioners, the gap between the theoretical analysis (global optimality) and practical training (local minima via SGD) is significant and should be addressed more thoroughly. The authors could strengthen this section by discussing under what conditions global optimality is achievable or by providing evidence that SGD solutions approximate global optima well in their experimental settings.

**Limited explanation of parallel NN architecture**: The paper does not provide sufficient intuition for why the parallel NN architecture is special or necessary for achieving the theoretical results. The authors state that "parallel NNs have been shown to be more well-behaved both theoretically and empirically" and cite several papers, but they do not explain at a high level what structural properties of parallel NNs enable the theoretical analysis. Specifically:
- Why can't the results extend to standard MLPs with block-diagonal weight structure?
- Is the parallel structure essential for the ℓp-sparsity equivalence in Proposition 4, or is it merely convenient for the proof?
- The comparison in Table 1 shows that parallel NNs are not the only architecture that can achieve near-minimax rates, so what is the fundamental advantage?

A clearer exposition of the architectural role would help readers understand whether the results represent fundamental insights about deep learning or are specific to this particular architecture choice.

**Restrictive assumptions on function classes**: The requirement α - d/p > 1 in Proposition 7 is stronger than the typical continuity condition α ≥ d/p found in approximation theory. While Remark 1 acknowledges this and provides a step function counterexample, the practical implications are not fully explored. This constraint limits the applicability of the results to lower-smoothness functions and should be discussed more prominently in the main text. Additionally, the condition α > d(1/p - 1/r)+ in Suzuki (2018) is mentioned as a comparison point, but a more detailed discussion of why the stronger condition is necessary here would be helpful.

### Presentation Issues

**Dense mathematical exposition**: The paper is quite technical, with heavy notation (Table 2 lists 15+ symbols) and intricate proofs deferred to appendices. While technical depth is appropriate for a theory paper, the main text could be more accessible by:
- Providing more intuitive explanations of key concepts before diving into technical definitions
- Using running examples (e.g., the Doppler function from experiments) to illustrate abstract concepts
- Breaking up long theorem statements with more interpretive text

For instance, Theorem 1 introduces many parameters (α, p, q, L, w, M) and conditions simultaneously. A step-by-step build-up with a simplified version first would aid comprehension.

**Insufficient motivation for Besov and BV spaces**: While the paper assumes familiarity with these function classes, many readers may not have deep background in classical nonparametric statistics. The connection to heterogeneous smoothness could be illustrated more concretely. The paper mentions that Besov spaces generalize Sobolev and Hölder spaces but doesn't explain why this generality matters for understanding neural networks. The brief description in Section 2.2 and Appendix C could be supplemented with more intuitive explanations or visual illustrations of functions in these classes.

**Limited discussion of practical implications**: The paper proves theoretical results under assumptions that may not hold in practice (global optimality, specific architecture). The experimental section (Section 5) provides some empirical validation, but the connection between theory and experiments could be stronger. For instance:
- How sensitive are the results to the choice of λ? The theory requires "proper choice" of λ depending on unknown parameters (α, d, L), but practitioners must choose λ via cross-validation. How does this affect performance?
- The experiments use relatively small sample sizes (256-1000) and low-dimensional inputs (d=1). How do the results scale to higher dimensions?
- Figure 3 shows that vanilla NN sometimes matches parallel NN performance, which seems to contra

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: The paper analyzes parallel neural networks for nonparametric regression (including approximation error and regression error analysis). Suppose the regression function has nonhomogeneous regularities, e.g., Besov functions or functions of bounded variation. Parallel neural networks trained with weight decay achieves near optimal estimation, indicating the adaptability of parallel neural networks.
reviews.strength_and_weaknesses:
============= Strength =============

The paper is clearly written with both theoretical and empirical results. The theoretical results are correct and empirical results are supportive.

The adaptability of neural networks is studied in many recent works, including its adaptability to data geometry and adaptability to structures in function spaces. This paper considers parallel neural networks and uses weight decay for controlling the complexity of the network, which circumvents sparsity constraints in many existing network approximation results.

============= Weakness =============

There is no related work section. Some of papers share similar ideas to parallel neural networks, for example, "Approximation and Non-parametric Estimation of ResNet-type Convolutional Neural Networks" and "Besov Function Approximation and Binary Classification on Low-Dimensional Manifolds Using Convolutional Residual Networks" both rely on implementing block sparse feedforward networks. The resulting (residual) convolutional network does not need a sparsity constraint in training.

The obtained rate of estimation is slightly slower than the optimal rate, which is attainable (up to log factors) in many recent works on using ReLU neural networks for nonparametric regression.

From experiments, parallel neural networks seem to achieve comparable performance to neural networks and conventional methods.
reviews.clarity_quality_novelty_and_reproducibil:
Some of the technical contributions need highlighting. For example, the conversion of weight decay objective to the constraint form in Equation (5) can be highlighted as a contribution -- if it does not appear in Parhi & Nowak (2021a). Another example is the decomposition of \mathcal{F}_{\parallel} and \mathcal{F}_{\perp}.

A discussion on why parallel neural networks can only achieve the optimal rate in the asymptotic sense ($L \to \infty$) is helpful. Is it because the weight decay introduces additional bias?

In Theorem 1, it is good to state how to choose weight decay $\lambda$.

Lemma 6 is relative difficult to understand, mainly because the first sentence begins with an equation.

Overall, the paper is well written. There are some typos and typeset issues, for example, "errorand" --> "error and" above Section 4.2
reviews.summary_of_the_review: I think the paper is interesting. I am currently giving a slightly negative rating, due to some concerns of the results and missing references. I am happy to raise to a positive rating after paper revision and an effective author feedback.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.

reviews.text:
# Expanded Peer Review

## Summary Of The Paper

The paper analyzes parallel neural networks for nonparametric regression, providing both approximation error and estimation error analysis. The core contribution addresses the fundamental question of whether neural networks can be locally adaptive—that is, whether they can optimally estimate functions with heterogeneous smoothness, such as those in Besov spaces or bounded variation (BV) classes. The authors demonstrate that parallel ReLU neural networks trained with standard weight decay (ℓ2 regularization) can achieve near-optimal estimation rates for such function classes. A key theoretical insight is that weight decay in this architecture is equivalent to promoting ℓp-sparsity (where p = 2/L) in the coefficients of learned basis functions, and the error rate approaches the minimax optimal rate as depth L increases.

## Strength And Weaknesses

### ============= Strength =============

**Clear presentation and comprehensive treatment.** The paper is clearly written with well-organized sections that guide the reader through both theoretical and empirical contributions. The main theorem (Theorem 1) is stated precisely with explicit conditions, and the proof strategy is decomposed into manageable pieces: equivalence to sparse regression (Section 4.1), estimation error analysis (Section 4.2), and approximation error analysis (Section 4.3). The empirical results in Section 5 effectively support the theoretical findings and demonstrate practical viability of the approach.

**Important theoretical contribution on local adaptivity.** The adaptability of neural networks to varying smoothness properties is studied in recent works, including adaptability to data geometry and function space structures. This paper makes a significant contribution by studying parallel neural networks with weight decay, which circumvents the need for explicit sparsity constraints during training—a requirement that plagued many existing network approximation results (e.g., Suzuki, 2018; Oono & Suzuki, 2019). The authors demonstrate that by tuning only the regularization factor λ, the network can adapt to different smoothness parameters α without requiring architecture modifications for each function class. This is a notable improvement over prior work where the network architecture had to be carefully chosen based on the target function class parameters.

**Novel equivalence result with practical implications.** Proposition 4 establishes that ℓ2 regularization in L-layer parallel networks is equivalent to ℓ2/L-norm penalization on learned coefficients. While the authors acknowledge this builds on prior work (Savarese et al., 2019; Burer & Monteiro, 2003), their application of this equivalence to prove learning bounds is novel and insightful. The equivalence provides intuition for why parallel networks with weight decay can achieve local adaptivity: the induced ℓp sparsity with p = 2/L becomes arbitrarily small as L increases, promoting increasingly sparse solutions that approximate the ℓ0 sparsity achievable by classical methods.

**Principled handling of unconstrained function subspaces.** The decomposition of the function class into F = F∥ × F⊥ (Proposition 15) where F⊥ is an unconstrained affine subspace and F∥ is compact, represents a technically non-trivial contribution. This decomposition allows the authors to separately bound the estimation error using covering number arguments (which apply only to compact sets) and the approximation error. The technique of handling the unconstrained subspace F⊥ through its finite dimension is elegant and may be of independent interest for analyzing other neural network architectures.

**Depth-dependent improvement toward optimality.** The result that error rates approach minimax optimality as L → ∞ (Corollary 2) provides theoretical justification for the empirical success of deep networks. Specifically, when L ≳ log n, the MSE becomes Õ(n^{-2α/(2α+d)(1-o(1))}), which matches the minimax rate up to logarithmic factors and an o(1) factor in the exponent. This depth-dependent improvement is more interpretable than many existing results and helps explain why practitioners prefer deeper architectures.

**Comprehensive technical analysis.** The paper includes detailed proofs with careful treatment of several non-trivial aspects: (1) bounding the covering number of parallel networks without explicit dependence on the number of subnetworks M (Theorem 5), (2) analyzing sparse approximation of Besov functions using B-spline bases with bounded ℓp coefficients (Proposition 7), and (3) approximating B-spline bases with neural networks while controlling weight norms (Lemma 11). These technical contributions strengthen the overall theoretical framework.

### ============= Weakness =============

**Missing related work section.** The paper lacks a dedicated related work section, which is a significant omission given the rich literature on neural network approximation and nonparametric regression. While Section A provides some discussion, key papers are scattered throughout the main text and appendix. Notably, several papers implement ideas similar to parallel neural networks that deserve more prominent discussion. For example, "Approximation and Non-parametric Estimation of ResNet-type Convolutional Neural Networks" (Oono & Suzuki, 2019) and "Besov Function Approximation and Binary Classification on Low-Dimensional Manifolds Using Convolutional Residual Networks" (Liu et al., 2021) both employ block-sparse feedforward networks and residual architectures that avoid explicit sparsity constraints during training. A systematic comparison of how the parallel NN approach relates to these residual network constructions would strengthen the positioning of this work. Additionally, the connection to the broader literature on implicit regularization and the role of overparameterization in neural networks could be explored more thoroughly.

**Suboptimal rate compared to recent work.** The obtained estimation rate in Theorem 1 is Õ(n^{-2α/d(1-2/L)/(2α/d+1-2/(pL))}), which is slightly slower than the minimax rate O(n^{-2α/(2α+d)}) by a factor of (1-2/L). While the authors argue this gap closes as L → ∞, this requirement that L must grow with n (specifically L ≳ log n to achieve near-optimality) represents an additional constraint not present in some recent work. For instance, Schmidt-Hieber (2020) achieves the minimax rate up to logarithmic factors with fixed depth, and Parhi & Nowak (2021c) achieve near-minimax rates for BV classes with fixed architecture. The necessity of increasing depth with sample size raises practical questions about the computational cost and optimization difficulty of training such deep networks. The paper would benefit from a more detailed discussion of whether this depth requirement is fundamental or an artifact of the analysis technique.

**Parallel networks are a restricted architecture.** While parallel networks have theoretical advantages, they represent a restricted class of neural networks compared to the standard feedforward architectures commonly used in practice. The requirement that all subnetworks receive the same input and their outputs are summed (Figure 2a) limits the architectural flexibility compared to standard deep networks where information can flow and transform through arbitrary connectivity patterns. The paper mentions that some successful architectures like SqueezeNet and ResNext use similar ideas, but a more thorough empirical comparison with standard architectures on realistic datasets would strengthen the practical relevance of the results. The experiments in Section 5 focus on one-dimensional synthetic functions; extending to higher dimensions and real datasets would be valuable.

**Experimental results are somewhat modest.** The numerical experiments compare parallel neural networks (PNN) against trend filtering, wavelet denoising, smoothing splines, and vanilla neural networks on one-dimensional synthetic functions (Doppler and "vary" functions). While the results show that PNN performs comparably to trend filtering and wavelet methods, the improvements are not dramatic. In Figure 3(b) and (e), there are "mild drops" in the best achievable MSE for PNN compared to trend filtering, which the authors attribute to PNN needing to learn basis functions that trend filtering has hard-coded. However, this raises the question of whether the additional computational complexity of training a neural network is justified by the marginal improvement in performance. Furthermore, all experiments are limited to one-dimensional problems with relatively small sample sizes (256-1000 samples). Extension to higher-dimensional problems would better demonstrate the practical advantages of the approach, particularly in settings where classical nonparametric methods become computationally intractable.

**Unclear practical guidance on hyperparameter selection.** While Lemma 17 provides an explicit (though implicit) choice of λ depending on the unknown target function and noise level, practical hyperparameter selection is mentioned only briefly: "Empirically, it can be determined empirically, e.g. using cross validation." The theoretical choice λ ≲ MSE(f̂)/P' depends on quantities that are not known a priori. The paper would benefit from more detailed guidance on how to select λ in practice, including discussion of whether standard cross-validation works well or if the landscape of the regularization path (Figure 4) presents challenges. The observation in

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper proposes to analyze the theory of deep neural network from the perspective of non-parametric regression. The authors show that training an $L$ layer neural network with weight decay is basically the same as penalized regression. And in the case of functions in the Besov space and bounded variation space, neural networks achieves almost near-optimal rates. When the number of layers increase, the error decreases and thus explains why deep networks performs better than shallow ones. The authors also validate some of their claims using experiments.
reviews.strength_and_weaknesses:
Strength:
  1. The paper is well-written and mostly clear.
  2. The theoretical analysis provided by the authors are, as far as I am aware of, novel and interesting.
  3. The obtained results do provide some insights on, for example, why deep networks do no overfit and why deep networks perform better than shallow networks.

Weaknesses:
  
 First off, I want to say that I am very new to the area of non-parametric regression and the function spaces the authors use in this paper. I have some experience with deep learning theory but this direction is completely new to me. Therefore, I might not be the best reviewer for this paper. Some of my comments might be unfair/wrong and I hope the authors and the other reviewers can help me understand this paper a little more during the discussion period. 

  Now back to this paper, I do have some questions about the setting and the results the authors provide. 
  
  1. The authors have chosen to optimize a set of parallel feedforward networks with $L_2$ regularization (I don't really like using the word "weight decay" here because there is no mentioning of the optimizer, e.g. SGD/Adam, and thus weight decay does not really exist). I wonder why the authors didn't include the regularization for the bias in Eqn. (2)? After looking at the Appendix, I think the authors do analyze the network with the bias term, so why is the regularization for bias missing? I think in practice, people have both regularization for weights and bias.

  2. In Theorem 1, I am confused by the statement "with proper choice of the parameter of weight decay $\lambda$". How large does the parameter need to be? Does it need to be time-varying, for example? If the choice of $\lambda$ needs to depend on the other parameters such as $M, L, p, q$, please state it explicitly in the theorems so that we can understand it more clearly.
  
  3. I am a little confused by the results in Corollary 2 and 3. For example, in Cor 2, although the $o(1)$ term is negligible, does it mean that the rate of deep neural network is worse than that of traditional nonparametric regression models? Are there models that can achieve the minimax rates that the authors have state Page 3? I think it might be better to have a table to compare the results in this paper with the results in the literature. Also, what is going to happen if $L > 100 C \log n$? For very deep neural networks, do the theorems still hold?

  4. For Cor 3, although I know that most of the paper is focused on $L\gg 2$, the authors might want to mention that the results here fail for $L=1, 2$. Also, the claim that "DNN has an advantage over kernels" is a little confusing, shouldn't there be an assumption on $L$ (L> some function of m) so that the bound for DNN is strictly better than that of kernels? I hope the authors can state it more clearly.

  I do not have enough time to go through all the proof details, but the proof overview section does give me enough intuition of how the theorems are derived. The experimental results are a good supplement to the theory.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
This paper proposes to analyze the theory of deep neural network from the perspective of non-parametric regression. The authors show that training an $L$ layer neural network with weight decay is basically the same as penalized regression. And in the case of functions in the Besov space and bounded variation space, neural networks achieves almost near-optimal rates. When the number of layers increase, the error decreases and thus explains why deep networks performs better than shallow ones. The authors also validate some of their claims using experiments.

Strength And Weaknesses:
Strength:
  1. The paper is well-written and mostly clear.
  2. The theoretical analysis provided by the authors are, as far as I am aware of, novel and interesting.
  3. The obtained results do provide some insights on, for example, why deep networks do no overfit and why deep networks perform better than shallow networks.

Weaknesses:
  
 First off, I want to say that I am very new to the area of non-parametric regression and the function spaces the authors use in this paper. I have some experience with deep learning theory but this direction is completely new to me. Therefore, I might not be the best reviewer for this paper. Some of my comments might be unfair/wrong and I hope the authors and the other reviewers can help me understand this paper a little more during the discussion period. 

  Now back to this paper, I do have some questions about the setting and the results the authors provide. 
  
  1. The authors have chosen to optimize a set of parallel feedforward networks with $L_2$ regularization (I don't really like using the word "weight decay" here because there is no mentioning of the optimizer, e.g. SGD/Adam, and thus weight decay does not really exist). I wonder why the authors didn't include the regularization for the bias in Eqn. (2)? After looking at the Appendix, I think the authors do analyze the network with the bias term, so why is the regularization for bias missing? I think in practice, people have both regularization for weights and bias.

  2. In Theorem 1, I am confused by the statement "with proper choice of the parameter of weight decay $\lambda$". How large does the parameter need to be? Does it need to be time-varying, for example? If the choice of $\lambda$ needs to depend on the other parameters such as $M, L, p, q$, please state it explicitly in the theorems so that we can understand it more clearly.
  
  3. I am a little confused by the results in Corollary 2 and 3. For example, in Cor 2, although the $o(1)$ term is negligible, does it mean that the rate of deep neural network is worse than that of traditional nonparametric regression models? Are there models that can achieve the minimax rates that the authors have state Page 3? I think it might be better to have a table to compare the results in this paper with the results in the literature. Also, what is going to happen if $L > 100 C \log n$? For very deep neural networks, do the theorems still hold?

  4. For Cor 3, although I know that most of the paper is focused on $L\gg 2$, the authors might want to mention that the results here fail for $L=1, 2$. Also, the claim that "DNN has an advantage over kernels" is a little confusing, shouldn't there be an assumption on $L$ (L> some function of m) so that the bound for DNN is strictly better than that of kernels? I hope the authors can state it more clearly.

  I do not have enough time to go through all the proof details, but the proof overview section does give me enough intuition of how the theorems are derived. The experimental results are a good supplement to the theory.

Clarity Quality Novelty And Reproducibility:
The paper is mostly clear, novel, and reproducible with the appendix.

Summary Of The Review:
Overall, this seems to be a good paper with nice contributions. However, I am confused by some settings and theoretical results of the authors. Again, I am no expert in this field, and if there is anything wrong in my review, please correct me and I am happy to increase my score.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
# Expanded Peer Review

## Summary Of The Paper

This paper proposes to analyze the theory of deep neural networks from the perspective of classical nonparametric regression. The authors show that training an L-layer parallel neural network with standard ℓ2 regularization (weight decay) is mathematically equivalent to solving a penalized regression problem with ℓp sparsity (where p = 2/L) on the coefficients of learned basis functions. Under this equivalence, the authors prove that such networks can achieve near-minimax optimal rates for estimating functions in Besov spaces and bounded variation (BV) spaces. A key finding is that as the depth L increases, the error rate approaches the minimax optimal rate, thus providing a theoretical explanation for why deeper networks perform better than shallower ones. The authors validate their theoretical findings with experiments on synthetic functions with heterogeneous smoothness.

## Strength And Weaknesses

### Strengths

1. **Well-written and clear presentation**: The paper is generally well-structured with a clear motivation rooted in classical nonparametric regression theory. The authors effectively explain the problem setup and provide good intuition for their main results. The use of Figure 1 to illustrate functions with heterogeneous smoothness and the parallel NN architecture diagrams in Figure 2 aid significantly in understanding the main contributions.

2. **Novel and interesting theoretical analysis**: The theoretical contributions appear to be novel and significant. The equivalence between ℓ2 regularized parallel neural networks and ℓp-sparse regression (Proposition 4) is elegant and provides new insights. More importantly, the authors develop new techniques to handle the covering number analysis of neural networks without explicit dependence on the number of subnetworks M, which is a technical contribution of independent interest. The decomposition in Equation (4) that separates the constrained subspace F∥ from the unconstrained subspace F⊥ and the novel analysis of each component represents a major technical advancement.

3. **Provides valuable theoretical insights**: The paper offers several important insights into why deep neural networks work well. The explanation for why depth helps (as formalized in Corollary 2, where the error rate approaches minimax as L ≳ log n) is particularly valuable. The observation that ℓ2 regularization induces ℓp sparsity with p = 2/L provides an intuitive explanation for how neural networks avoid overfitting even when overparameterized. Additionally, the comparison with kernel methods (Corollary 3) demonstrates that deeper networks can achieve strictly better rates than linear estimators, separating DNNs from kernel methods in a principled way.

4. **Comprehensive theoretical treatment**: The paper provides a complete analysis combining approximation error (via B-spline basis decomposition) and estimation error (via covering number bounds). The treatment of both Besov and BV function classes, along with the embedding relationships between them (Equation 1), shows breadth of applicability. The authors also provide results for both fixed design (main results) and random design (Theorem 19) settings, demonstrating thoroughness.

### Weaknesses

#### Major Concerns

1. **Regularization of bias terms and architectural choices**: The authors choose not to regularize the bias terms b_j^(ℓ) in Equation (2), stating they do this "to provide a cleaner equivalent model." While they mention in the main text that regularizing bias would yield "similar results," this choice deserves more justification. In practice, weight decay in deep learning frameworks typically applies to all parameters, and the theoretical implications of excluding bias regularization are not fully explored. The authors do state in the appendix that if bias terms are regularized, "the result will be similar," but this should be quantified more precisely. How does excluding bias regularization affect the constant factors in the bounds? Does it change the dependence on other parameters? Additionally, the restriction to parallel neural networks is significant—while the authors argue these architectures are "more well-behaved theoretically," the restriction limits the applicability of the results to standard feedforward architectures commonly used in practice.

2. **Hyperparameter selection and practical implementation**: The statement "with proper choice of the parameter of regularization λ that depends on D, α, d, L" in Theorem 1 is vague and raises important practical questions. The authors defer the explicit choice to Lemma 17, but the dependency on unknown quantities is problematic. Specifically:
   - The choice of λ depends on the minimax rate and MSE of the optimal function in the constrained problem, which are unknown a priori in practice.
   - The authors state λ ≲ MSE(f̂)/P′ ≲ n^(-(1-2/L)), but this circular dependency (λ depends on the MSE of the solution, which in turn depends on λ) requires careful handling.
   - While the authors mention that λ can be determined empirically using cross-validation, no guidance is provided on how to do this effectively or how sensitive the results are to the choice of λ.
   - The assumption in Lemma 17 that there exists a smooth relationship between λ and the regularizer norm is non-trivial and may not hold in practice, particularly when dealing with non-convex optimization landscapes.

3. **Gap between theory and practice regarding the minimax rate**: In Corollary 2, the authors achieve a rate of Õ(n^(-2α/(2α+d)(1-o(1)))), where the o(1) factor is O(1/log(n)). While this approaches the minimax rate of O(n^(-2α/(2α+d))), there is still a gap. The authors should be more explicit about:
   - The magnitude of the o(1) factor for practical values of n (e.g., n = 100, 1000, 10000).
   - Whether this gap is fundamental to the approach or if it can be improved with different techniques.
   - How the polynomial dependence on (w^(4-4/L)L^(2-4/L))/n^(1-2/L) in Theorem 1 affects the practical applicability of the bounds. For moderate values of n and reasonable network widths, could these constant factors be prohibitively large?

4. **Conditions on network width and depth**: Theorem 1 requires w ≥ O(m d) where m = ⌈α⌉ - 1. This means:
   - For smooth functions (large α), the required width grows substantially.
   - The width requirement depends on α, but in practice α is unknown and must be estimated or upper-bounded.
   - The authors claim the network can adapt to different smoothness parameters by only tuning λ, but if the width is fixed based on an estimated upper bound of α, how does this affect the adaptive properties? For instance, if we overestimate α and set w = O(ᾱ d) where ᾱ > α, does this introduce additional constant factors or affect convergence rates?
   - The requirement M ≳ n^((1-2/L)/(2α/d+1-2/(pL))) means the number of subnetworks must grow with n. For fixed M, what can we say about the convergence rate?

5. **Limited discussion of the approximation-estimation tradeoff**: While the authors decompose the MSE into approximation and estimation error, the interplay between these terms could be discussed more thoroughly. Specifically:
   - In Theorem 1, how is the optimal choice of M (or equivalently P′) determined in practice?
   - The choice M ≂ (n^(1-2/L)/(w^(4-4/L)L^(2-4/L)))^(1/(2α/d+1-2/(pL))) depends on unknown quantities like α. How sensitive is the bound to errors in estimating this quantity?
   - The exponential term e^(-c₆L) in the approximation error bound becomes negligible for L ≳ log(1/ε), but for practical values of L, is this term significant?

#### Medium Concerns

6. **Clarity on advantages over existing methods**: In Corollary 3, the authors claim that DNNs have an advantage over kernel methods for estimating BV^(m) functions. However, the statement needs clarification:
   - The comparison is with "any linear estimators," which achieve O(n^(-(2m+1)/(2m+2))).
   - The DNN rate from Corollary 3 is Õ(n^(-((2m+2)(1-2/L))/(2m+3-2/L))).
   - For the DNN rate to be strictly better than the linear rate, we need (2m+2)(1-2/L)/(2m+3-2/L) > (2m+1)/(2m+2).
   - The authors state this holds "when L > O(

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper extends Suzuki’s work (ICLR2018) to parallel neural networks in the Besov space as well as BV functions, studies the generalization properties in terms of approximation error via B-spline basis and estimation error via covering number, and demonstrates the $\ell_2$ norm (weight decay) in parallel ReLU neural networks is equivalent to a certain sparse regularization term.
reviews.strength_and_weaknesses:
**Pros:** 

1. This work allows for over-parameterization without the extra sparsity condition
The obtained bound of the covering number is independent of the number of subnetworks, which is substituted by the Frobenius norm.  

**Cons:**
 
1. The motivation on locally adaptivity makes sense, comparing linear estimators and DNNs. Nevertheless, the motivation on the comparison of (Suzuki ICLR2019)  is relatively weak. The authors claim that Suzuki’s work requires certain conditions on the width, the depth, the sparsity to achieve the minimax rate on deep ReLU networks, which is different from practical uses. Nevertheless, this work does not focus on this issue but centers around another architecture, i.e., parallel neural networks. Even though parallel neural networks have theoretical and empirical applications as the authors suggested, the “correct” motivation is to solve certain conditions in Suzuki’s work under the same architecture.

More importantly, this work also requires several conditions on the width, the depth, etc. In the “No architecture tuning” part, the required architecture of the model still depends on $\alpha$, see Theorem 1. It is hard for me to accept the authors’ motivation.


2. In theorem 1, the number of subnetworks is exponentially large enough w.r.t the input dimension $d$. This is unacceptable in both theory and practice.


3. In Proposition 4, the equivalence between Eq. (2) and Eq. (12) is imprecise via the Lagrangian’s method in optimization. The constraint value P doesn't appear in Eq. (2) and it is also not clear how the dual variable is handled. In fact P (as well as P’) is unknown or uncomputable, which also makes the upper bound of the covering number appear infeasible in Theorem 5 depending on P’. It is ok for ease of description but is not rigorous in a theory paper corresponding to the first contribution.

4. After reading this paper, I think the technical contribution is to derive the covering number bound of the parallel neural networks for the estimation error, i.e., Theorem 5 and Lemma 6. The proof for approximation error follows Yarotsky (2017); Suzuki (2018). However, this paper works under a fixed design, which is much easier to derive the estimation error.
reviews.clarity_quality_novelty_and_reproducibil:
This paper is well-written. Apart from the above mentioned issues, I have other questions that

1. I agree with the authors that kernel methods, e.g., NTK, lacks local adaptivity to some extent due to RKHS. I’m wondering that, the mean field regime (instead of NTK) is also suboptimal to local adaptivity?


2. For the random design, the proof of estimation error can be similarly established by [1]?

[1] Schmidt-Hieber, Johannes. "Nonparametric regression using deep neural networks with ReLU activation function." The Annals of Statistics 48, no. 4 (2020): 1875-1897.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: This work has some good results on the sparsity, the separation between linear estimators and DNNs in Besov spaces as well as BV functions. Nevertheless, the derived results appear vacuous in terms of imprecise Lagrangian dual and curse of dimensionality of the input dimension.

**Cons:**
 
1. The motivation on locally adaptivity makes sense, comparing linear estimators and DNNs. Nevertheless, the motivation on the comparison of (Suzuki ICLR2019)  is relatively weak. The authors claim that Suzuki’s work requires certain conditions on the width, the depth, the sparsity to achieve the minimax rate on deep ReLU networks, which is different from practical uses. Nevertheless, this work does not focus on this issue but centers around another architecture, i.e., parallel neural networks. Even though parallel neural networks have theoretical and empirical applications as the authors suggested, the “correct” motivation is to solve certain conditions in Suzuki’s work under the same architecture.

More importantly, this work also requires several conditions on the width, the depth, etc. In the “No architecture tuning” part, the required architecture of the model still depends on $\alpha$, see Theorem 1. It is hard for me to accept the authors’ motivation.


2. In theorem 1, the number of subnetworks is exponentially large enough w.r.t the input dimension $d$. This is unacceptable in both theory and practice.


3. In Proposition 4, the equivalence between Eq. (2) and Eq. (12) is imprecise via the Lagrangian’s method in optimization. The constraint value P doesn't appear in Eq. (2) and it is also not clear how the dual variable is handled. In fact P (as well as P’) is unknown or uncomputable, which also makes the upper bound of the covering number appear infeasible in Theorem 5 depending on P’. It is ok for ease of description but is not rigorous in a theory paper corresponding to the first contribution.

The proof for approximation error follows Yarotsky (2017); Suzuki (2018). However, this paper works under a fixed design, which is much easier to derive the estimation error.

Clarity Quality Novelty And Reproducibility:
This paper is well-written. Apart from the above mentioned issues, I have other questions that

1. I agree with the authors that kernel methods, e.g., NTK, lacks local adaptivity to some extent due to RKHS. I’m wondering that, the mean field regime (instead of NTK) is also suboptimal to local adaptivity?

reviews.text:
# Expanded Peer Review

## Summary Of The Paper

This paper extends Suzuki's work (ICLR 2018) to parallel neural networks in the Besov space as well as bounded variation (BV) functions. The authors study the generalization properties by decomposing the mean squared error into approximation error (via B-spline basis decomposition) and estimation error (via covering number analysis). A key contribution is demonstrating that the standard ℓ₂ norm regularization (weight decay) in parallel ReLU neural networks is equivalent to a sparse ℓₚ regularization term on the coefficients of learned basis functions, where p = 2/L for an L-layer network.

## Strengths

1. **Overparameterization without explicit sparsity constraints**: This work makes a significant contribution by allowing for over-parameterization without requiring practitioners to explicitly impose sparsity constraints on the network weights. The obtained bound on the covering number is notably independent of the number of subnetworks M, which is instead controlled through the Frobenius norm of the weights. This is a meaningful technical advancement over prior work, as it decouples the complexity measure from the number of subnetwork components.

2. **Novel equivalence between weight decay and ℓₚ-sparse regression**: The connection established in Proposition 4 between standard ℓ₂ regularization and ℓₚ-sparse regression with learned representations is elegant and provides valuable theoretical insight. The observation that p = 2/L decreases toward zero as depth increases offers an intuitive explanation for why deeper networks can achieve sparser solutions. This equivalence, while building on techniques from prior work (Savarese et al., 2019), is cleverly leveraged to prove new learning bounds.

3. **Theoretical separation from kernel methods**: The paper makes progress toward understanding a fundamental question about why DNNs outperform kernel methods. By demonstrating that weight-decayed parallel ReLU networks can achieve near-minimax rates for locally adaptive function classes (Besov and BV), while kernel methods fundamentally cannot (due to fixed RKHS structure), the work provides theoretical justification for this empirical phenomenon.

4. **Comprehensive theoretical analysis**: The decomposition of MSE into estimation and approximation errors is thorough. The approximation error analysis via B-spline basis functions (Lemma 11, Proposition 7, Theorem 8) is detailed and builds systematically from univariate to multivariate cases. The handling of the unconstrained subspace in Proposition 15 represents a non-trivial technical contribution to the statistical learning machinery.

5. **Experimental validation**: The numerical experiments on synthetic functions (Doppler and "vary" functions) with heterogeneous smoothness demonstrate that parallel neural networks achieve performance comparable to specialized methods like trend filtering and wavelet denoising, which are known to be minimax-optimal for BV classes.

## Major Weaknesses

### 1. Weak Motivation Relative to Prior Work

The motivation comparing this work to Suzuki (2018) is not entirely convincing. The authors claim that Suzuki's approach requires careful tuning of architecture parameters (width, depth, sparsity level) based on the function class parameters, which is impractical. However, rather than addressing these limitations within the same architecture, the authors instead pivot to studying parallel neural networks—a fundamentally different architecture. This strategic choice, while theoretically interesting, does not directly resolve the stated problem with Suzuki's framework.

More problematically, **this work also imposes non-trivial architectural requirements**. According to Theorem 1, the width must satisfy w ≥ O(md) where m = ⌈α - 1⌉. This means the architecture still depends explicitly on α, the smoothness parameter of the Besov class. The authors claim in the "Adaptivity to function spaces" section (after Theorem 1) that "neural networks can adapt to smoothness parameter by tuning only the regularization parameter," but this is misleading. The width requirement w = O(md) still depends on α, so practitioners must either know or overestimate α when designing the network. This undermines the claimed advantage over Suzuki's approach.

Furthermore, the paper does not address why one should consider parallel neural networks specifically for this problem. While the authors cite theoretical and empirical advantages of parallel architectures (citing Haeffele & Vidal, 2017; Ergen & Pilanci, 2021b;c;d), the connection between these general advantages and the specific problem of local adaptivity in nonparametric regression is not clearly established. A more compelling motivation would either: (a) prove that standard DNNs cannot achieve these rates, or (b) provide strong empirical evidence that parallel NNs substantially outperform standard NNs on these tasks.

### 2. Exponential Dependence on Input Dimension

In Theorem 1, the required number of subnetworks is M ≳ n^(1-2/L) / (2α/d+1-2/(pL)). For fixed α, L, and p, as the input dimension d increases, the exponent (1-2/L)/(2α/d+1-2/(pL)) approaches (1-2/L)/(1-2/(pL)), but the denominator 2α/d approaches 0, making the exponent grow. This means M must grow as a polynomial (or worse) in n for high-dimensional problems.

More critically, examining the width requirement w = O(md) reveals that the width scales linearly with dimension d. The covering number bound in Theorem 5 is:
$$\log N(\mathcal{F}, δ) ≲ w^{2+2/(1-2/L)} L^2 \sqrt{d} P' \cdot δ^{-2/L/(1-2/L)}$$

The dependence on √d in the covering number, combined with w ∝ d, yields a dimension-dependent bound that scales as d^{1+2/(1-2/L)}. For L = 3, this becomes roughly d^4. This exponential-in-dimension behavior is problematic and represents a significant limitation in the practical applicability of the results.

The authors do not adequately discuss this curse of dimensionality. While the paper focuses on 1D experiments and briefly mentions that results extend to d-dimensional domains, the exponential dimension dependence is a fundamental barrier to applying these theoretical guarantees in realistic high-dimensional settings. This is particularly concerning given that modern applications of neural networks are predominantly high-dimensional.

### 3. Imprecision in the Equivalence Result (Proposition 4)

Proposition 4 claims equivalence between the regularized problem (Eq. 2) and the constrained problem (Eq. 6), but the statement and connection are not sufficiently rigorous for a theory paper. Several issues arise:

**Lagrangian duality and constraint values**: The equivalence is stated as "there exists a one-to-one mapping between λ > 0 and λ' > 0," but the mechanism of this mapping is not explicitly provided. In standard constrained optimization, the relationship between Lagrange multipliers and constraints is given by duality theory, yet the paper does not formally invoke this. The constraint value P' in Eq. (6) appears in the constrained formulation but has no explicit counterpart in Eq. (2). How exactly does one compute the appropriate λ for a desired P'?

**Uncomputable constraint values**: More fundamentally, P' (and by extension P) is unknown and uncomputable a priori. The constraint P' = ∑ⱼ |aⱼ|^(2/L) is not known until after solving the optimization problem. Yet Theorem 5's covering number bound depends on P'. This creates a circularity: to apply Theorem 5, one needs to know P', but P' is only determined by the solution. While the authors address this to some extent via Lemma 17 (which relates the regularized and constrained problems), the treatment remains informal.

**Practical feasibility**: For practitioners or even for rigorous theoretical analysis, it is unclear how to work with these bounds. The proof of Proposition 4 in Section D.1 relies on the AM-GM inequality and the observation that optimal solutions have norm-equalized weights across layers. While this is clever, the equivalence should be stated more carefully, perhaps by explicitly constructing the Lagrangian and proving the KKT conditions are satisfied, rather than relying on an implicit equivalence argument.

### 4. Limited Technical Novelty in Approximation Error Analysis

The paper correctly acknowledges that the approximation error analysis largely follows techniques from Yarotsky (2017) and Suzuki (2018). Lemma 11 on approximating B-spline bases with neural networks is a relatively straightforward application of these prior techniques to the parallel NN setting. Proposition 7 on sparse approximation of Besov functions is adapted from D

**Pros:**

**Cons:**

The authors claim that Suzuki’s work requires certain conditions on the width, the depth, the sparsity to achieve the minimax rate on deep ReLU networks, which is different from practical uses. Nevertheless, this work does not focus on this issue but centers around another architecture, i.e., parallel neural networks. Even though parallel neural networks have theoretical and empirical applications as the authors suggested, the “correct” motivation is to solve certain conditions in Suzuki’s work under the same architecture.

More importantly, this work also requires several conditions on the width, the depth, etc. In the “No architecture tuning” part, the required architecture of the model still depends on $\alpha$, see Theorem 1. It is hard for me to accept the authors’ motivation.


2. In theorem 1, the number of subnetworks is exponentially large enough w.r.t the input dimension $d$. This is unacceptable in both theory and practice.


3. In Proposition 4, the equivalence between Eq. (2) and Eq. (12) is imprecise via the Lagrangian’s method in optimization. The constraint value P doesn't appear in Eq. (2) and it is also not clear how the dual variable is handled. In fact P (as well as P’) is unknown or uncomputable, which also makes the upper bound of the covering number appear infeasible in Theorem 5 depending on P’. It is ok for ease of description but is not rigorous in a theory paper corresponding to the first contribution.

4. After reading this paper, I think the technical contribution is to derive the covering number bound of the parallel neural networks for the estimation error, i.e., Theorem 5 and Lemma 6. The proof for approximation error follows Yarotsky (2017); Suzuki (2018). However, this paper works under a fixed design, which is much easier to derive the estimation error.

Clarity Quality Novelty And Reproducibility:
This paper is well-written. Apart from the above mentioned issues, I have other questions that

1. I agree with the authors that kernel methods, e.g., NTK, lacks local adaptivity to some extent due to RKHS. I’m wondering that, the mean field regime (instead of NTK) is also suboptimal to local adaptivity?


2. For the random design, the proof of estimation error can be similarly established by [1]?

[1] Schmidt-Hieber, Johannes. "Nonparametric regression using deep neural networks with ReLU activation function." The Annals of Statistics 48, no. 4 (2020): 1875-1897.

Summary Of The Review:
This work has some good results on the sparsity, the separation between linear estimators and DNNs in Besov spaces as well as BV functions. Nevertheless, the derived results appear vacuous in terms of imprecise Lagrangian dual and curse of dimensionality of the input dimension.

Rating:
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold

reviews.text:
# Expanded Peer Review: Tensor-Based Sketching Method for Low-Rank Approximation of Data Streams

## Summary of the Paper

In this work, the authors propose a novel approach to the problem of low-rank matrix approximation in data streams by leveraging tensor decomposition techniques. Building upon previous sketching-based algorithms for randomized SVD, the authors introduce a tensor-based framework that exploits the structural relationships among multiple matrices in a data stream. The key innovation is to view a collection of matrices {A_d} from a data stream as a single third-order tensor and then apply Tucker decomposition to obtain an optimal sketching matrix S. This approach is motivated by a subspace perspective and aims to find a sketching matrix that approximates the top-k subspace common to all training matrices. The authors provide both a single-sided tensor-based algorithm (using Tucker1 decomposition) and a two-sided extension (using Tucker2 decomposition), along with theoretical justification and comprehensive experimental validation on three real-world datasets.

## Strengths

**Novel perspective and elegant methodology**: The core idea of viewing a stream of matrices as a tensor and leveraging tensor decomposition to find optimal sketching matrices is genuinely novel and insightful. This perspective naturally captures the inherent structure and correlations among matrices in a data stream, which previous methods fail to fully exploit. The connection between the proposed loss function (Equation 3.1) and the Tucker decomposition problem (Equation 3.3) is particularly elegant and well-motivated.

**Strong theoretical foundation**: The paper provides theoretical analysis through Theorem 1 and Theorem 2 that justifies the proposed approach. Theorem 1 establishes that the proposed loss function (Equation 3.1) is a valid relaxation of the IVY loss function (Equation 2.1), providing solid theoretical grounding for the method. The authors clearly demonstrate how their optimization problem relates to and improves upon previous formulations, which strengthens the contribution.

**Significant practical improvements**: The experimental results demonstrate substantial advantages over existing methods. The tensor-based algorithm achieves 0.55-0.64× lower test error compared to the best baselines on the HSI and Logo datasets, while being dramatically faster in training (0.53s vs. 46.55s on HSI, and 4.76s vs. 447.67s on Logo). These improvements are not marginal but represent orders of magnitude speedup, which is highly significant for practical applications. The ability to avoid expensive data normalization preprocessing further enhances the practical utility.

**Comprehensive experimental evaluation**: The paper includes thorough experiments on three diverse datasets (HSI, Logo, MRI) with multiple baselines including IVY, Few-Shot variants, and Butterfly networks. The authors also provide additional analysis of the two-sided algorithm and examine performance under different training sample ratios (2%, 20%, 80%), demonstrating robustness and scalability of the approach. The comparison with both sparse and dense variants of baseline methods shows careful consideration of implementation details.

**Practical algorithmic contributions**: Beyond the main tensor-based approach, the authors develop the two-sided SCW algorithm (Algorithm 3) which replaces the full SVD with QR decomposition for improved efficiency. This demonstrates thoughtful algorithm design and understanding of practical computational bottlenecks. The two-sided algorithm provides a useful speed-accuracy tradeoff for practitioners.

## Weaknesses

**Incomplete theoretical analysis**: While the paper provides Theorems 1 and 2, the theoretical analysis lacks important details that would strengthen the contribution. Most critically, the paper does not provide bounds on how close the obtained sketching matrix S is to the ideal U_k (the top-k left singular vectors) in general cases. Theorem 2 assumes that S already satisfies ∥U_k U_k^T - S^T S∥²_F < ε, but the paper does not characterize what ε is achieved by the proposed method or how it depends on properties of the data stream. This is a significant gap because understanding the quality of the Tucker decomposition solution is essential for understanding the overall approximation quality.

**Vague and unclear bounds in Theorem 2**: The bound in Theorem 2 (Equation 3.5) is stated as O(ε)∥A∥²_F, but the notation O(·) obscures important constants and dependencies. Specifically: (1) What are the hidden constants in the O(ε) term? (2) How does the bound depend on the rank r, the sketching dimension k, and the matrix dimensions m and n? (3) Is this bound tight, and how does it compare to bounds for other methods? The paper would benefit greatly from a more explicit statement of the bound with clear dependence on all relevant parameters. For instance, does the error grow with m and n, and if so, how does this compare to the guarantees of the SCW algorithm with random sketches?

**Insufficient characterization of the Tucker decomposition solution**: The paper claims that solving problem 3.3 yields an optimal sketching matrix S, but provides limited analysis of when and how well this solution approximates the common subspace of all training matrices. The assumption that S obtained from Tucker1 decomposition is "close in space to U_k" (stated informally in Section 3.1) needs rigorous justification. Under what conditions on the training data distribution is this assumption valid? How does the approximation quality degrade when training and test matrices come from different distributions?

**Limited clarity on problem formulation**: In the original problem statement (Equation 1.1), the notation is somewhat ambiguous. The formulation uses B_d to denote the low-rank approximation of A_d, but it is not immediately clear whether B_d is a separate variable for each d, or whether the authors intend to use a single low-rank approximation strategy across all d. This ambiguity extends to the loss functions presented later (e.g., Equation 2.1 and 3.1), where the relationship between individual matrix approximations and the aggregate loss could be more explicitly stated. Clearer notation distinguishing between per-matrix and aggregate objectives would improve readability.

**Memory requirements and scalability concerns**: While the paper addresses training time and testing time, the memory requirement limitation is mentioned only briefly in Section 4. The authors note that "it is required to load the whole training tensor at once" but argue that "a small number of training matrices are enough to achieve good low-rank approximation." However, this argument is not fully convincing. For truly massive data streams where the tensor cannot fit in memory, this method may not be applicable. The paper would benefit from a more thorough discussion of memory complexity and guidance on when the method is suitable. Additionally, Figure 3 shows that performance improvements plateau quickly (e.g., on MRI, the test error only decreases by a factor of 0.684 when sample_ratio increases from 2% to 80%), which suggests that the method may not leverage large training sets effectively. This behavior deserves more analysis.

**Dense sketching matrix computational tradeoff**: The tensor-based algorithm produces a dense sketching matrix S, which leads to faster training but slower testing compared to sparse baselines (unless specialized GPU operations like torch.matmul are available). While the paper addresses this by implementing dense baselines for fair comparison, the practical implications are not fully discussed. In scenarios where testing is more frequent than training, or where GPU acceleration is unavailable, the sparse methods might be preferable. The paper should provide clearer guidance on when the dense approach is advantageous and discuss potential solutions (e.g., quantization or pruning of the dense matrix).

**Limited analysis of failure modes and data distribution assumptions**: The paper assumes that matrices in the data stream have "strong relevance" (mentioned in Section 3.1), but this assumption is never formally defined or empirically validated. What happens when this assumption is violated? The MRI dataset shows weaker improvements when training data increases, which the authors attribute to "less strong relevance," but this explanation is speculative. A more rigorous characterization of when the method works well and when it fails would strengthen the contribution.

**Comparison fairness and baseline implementation details**: While the authors implement dense versions of baselines for fair comparison, some concerns remain. The sparse baselines use a specific sparse format and computation method (updating SA[l_i, j] directly), which may not be optimal. The comparison in Figure 2 shows that dense baselines achieve comparable accuracy (0.0191 on HSI vs. 0.0198 for the tensor method), suggesting that the advantage may not be as large as initially presented. More details on baseline hyperparameter tuning and optimization would help assess fairness.

## Clarity, Quality, Novelty, and Reproducibility

**Clarity**: The paper is generally well-written and organized, with clear motivation and algorithm descriptions. However, as noted above, some definitions (particularly in Section 1 regarding the problem formulation) could be more precise. The mathematical notation is mostly standard, though the use of ∥·∥²_F in some places could be more carefully distinguished from ∥·∥_F. The paper would also benefit from a more explicit discussion of the connection between the subspace perspective an

reviews.summary_of_the_paper:
The paper studies the data-driven low-rank approximation problem on data streams, which has gained attention recently. For a single matrix, some fast algorithms for low-rank approximation are based on sketching, and specifically they choose a sparse sketching matrix with random signs, which is multiplied by the original matrix to reduce its row and/or column dimensions while still allowing approximate recovery of the best low-rank approximation. In a stream of matrices assumed to be related to each other, it has been recently shown that the entries of the sketching matrix can be chosen in a data-driven way based on matrices previously seen on the stream instead of using random signs, in order to improve performance.

This paper suggests a new algorithm along these lines, that views the stream of matrices as a tensor of order 3, which leads to a formulation of a loss problem that can be solved directly and yields an appropriate sketching matrix, albeit non-sparse.
reviews.strength_and_weaknesses:
The tensor-based approach seems interesting and may lead to useful insights on the problem. However, there is a major issue that makes the proposed algorithm incomparable to prior work, both theoretically and empirically - the lack of sparsity of the output sketching matrix. This unfortunately renders the paper methodologically unviable. 

In all prior work (both oblivious and data-driven), the sketching matrix has a certain structure that allows fast matrix multiplication by it (usually sparsity), while here no such structure is guaranteed. On the theoretical level, this makes the resulting low-rank approximation algorithm asymptotically slower than all of the prior work it is compared to.

The manuscript is aware of this issue, and finally addresses it on the empirical level, on page 8, stating that even though the asymptotic running time is slower, the proposed algorithm is in fact empirically faster than prior work based on experiments. It goes on to explain this is because the implementation of the proposed algorithm uses the built-in (highly optimized) PyTorch matrix multiplication functionality, while the baselines were implemented with the non-optimized (and inevitably much slower) sparse matrix multiplication code released by the authors of those works. This unfortunately renders the reported testing time results essentially meaningless: the algorithms should be compared in the same experimental setting (software and hardware). Using the same dense matrix multiplication functionality in PyTorch for the sparse baselines would have already improved their running time to the same as the proposed method, erasing any reported advantage in testing time.

To be clear, it is true that as of now, PyTorch and comparable optimized software packages for matrices do not exploit sparsity, and dense matrix multiplication is as fast as sparse on the datasets considered here. Sparsity is useful theoretically (for getting better asymptotic bounds) and also in practice if the matrices are too large to fit in working memory (which makes dense matrix multiplication slow or infeasible). I do not question the reported results themselves, and one could perhaps make the argument that the sparsity of the sketching matrix is not helpful at all and could be dispensed with, but the current framing and methodological design of the paper are unviable. If sparsity is indeed unhelpful, then the sparse baseline should be implemented as dense ones and not crippled by attempting to leverage sparsity, and dense methods for low-rank approximation could be considered and reported as well (even full SVD).
reviews.clarity_quality_novelty_and_reproducibil:
Clarity: I found the proofs abbreviated and difficult to follow, and it would help to expand them and add explanations and bridging steps.
Reproducibility: I appreciate the fully disclosed details of the implementation.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: This is a potentially interesting paper but with flaws in its framing and methodological design, with which I cannot recommend acceptance. I could have recommended accepting a version of the paper with largely the same technical material, but that clarifies properly the departure from prior work, and with a viable experimental design that compares the baselines on a level ground and includes more relevant baselines. I also recommend rewriting the proofs in a clearer way that would assist in verifying the stated theorems.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.full_review_text:

In a stream of matrices assumed to be related to each other, it has been recently shown that the entries of the sketching matrix can be chosen in a data-driven way based on matrices previously seen on the stream instead of using random signs, in order to improve performance.

This paper suggests a new algorithm along these lines, that views the stream of matrices as a tensor of order 3, which leads to a formulation of a loss problem that can be solved directly and yields an appropriate sketching matrix, albeit non-sparse.

Strength And Weaknesses:
The tensor-based approach seems interesting and may lead to useful insights on the problem. However, there is a major issue that makes the proposed algorithm incomparable to prior work, both theoretically and empirically - the lack of sparsity of the output sketching matrix. This unfortunately renders the paper methodologically unviable. 

In all prior work (both oblivious and data-driven), the sketching matrix has a certain structure that allows fast matrix multiplication by it (usually sparsity), while here no such structure is guaranteed. This unfortunately renders the reported testing time results essentially meaningless: the algorithms should be compared in the same experimental setting (software and hardware). Using the same dense matrix multiplication functionality in PyTorch for the sparse baselines would have already improved their running time to the same as the proposed method, erasing any reported advantage in testing time.

I do not question the reported results themselves, and one could perhaps make the argument that the sparsity of the sketching matrix is not helpful at all and could be dispensed with, but the current framing and methodological design of the paper are unviable. Clarity Quality Novelty And Reproducibility:
Clarity: I found the proofs abbreviated and difficult to follow, and it would help to expand them and add explanations and bridging steps.
Reproducibility: I appreciate the fully disclosed details of the implementation.

Summary Of The Review:
This is a potentially interesting paper but with flaws in its framing and methodological design, with which I cannot recommend acceptance. I could have recommended accepting a version of the paper with largely the same technical material, but that clarifies properly the departure from prior work, and with a viable experimental design that compares the baselines on a level ground and includes more relevant baselines. I also recommend rewriting the proofs in a clearer way that would assist in verifying the stated theorems.
