Summary Of The Review:
The idea of encouraging the consistency between foreground prediction and instance mask prediction is good, it helps address the long-existing problems in the benchmark datasets and even helps alleviate the annotation burden in the future. Also, the experiments show the proposed method is effective and help boost the performance over the state-of-the-art. However, I am unsure if the consistency loss framework is significant enough to outstand peers.

Rating:
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold

title: Learning Interpretable Dynamics from Images of a Freely Rotating 3D Rigid Body

reviews.summary_of_the_paper:
The paper propose a method to learn dynamics and make predictions from sequences of images of 3D rigid bodies. The method incorporates physics priors by using SO(3) as the latent space, and by translating the problem of learning the dynamics to that of learning a (reduced) Hamiltonian. Predictions are then made by and integrating the kinematic equations on SO(3), and Lie-Poisson equations on the Lie coalgebra of SO(3).

They perform experiments with a synthetic dataset consisting of sequences of rotating rigid bodies with different shapes and different, diagonal and non-diagonal, inertia tensors.
reviews.strength_and_weaknesses:
The methods makes good use of well-known classical mechanics results about freely rotating rigid bodies giving good theoretical reasons for why the method should work. Interpretability of the learned dynamics is of utmost importance when using machine learning to model dynamical systems and the proposed algorithm gives very interpretable results constraining the dynamics on SO(3) and leaving all the unknown and irrelevant characteristics, such as color and shape of the modelled object, to the autoencoder.

Unfortunately, as stated in the paper, there aren't many datasets to assess performances for the particular problem tackled in this paper. 
The dataset could also represent a good benchmark for future works if made available, and therefore is a notable contribution.
The comparison with LSTMs and Neural ODEs is really meaningful as these methods are not as domain-specific as the proposed algorithms but certainly show how inductive biases like the one proposed in this paper are the way to go. Is there any 

Apart from the dynamics, the SO(3) latent space seems to facilitate the reconstruction: perhaps a pre-training of just the encoder-decoder architecture, which is not unrealistic for real-world application, would boost performances. Or perhaps you already do that.
reviews.clarity_quality_novelty_and_reproducibil:
The background section is very concise but sufficiently clear for readers that are familiar with Hamiltonian formulation of rigid bodies dynamics.
As claimed by the authors, and to the best of my knowledge it is the first physics-informed deep learning algorithm for predicting image sequences of 3D rigid bodies.

A few questions:

How figure 4 should be interpreted? I struggle to understand why the predicted $S^{2}\times S^{2}$ parameterisation of SO(3) is so different from the ground truth. In this setting there is a lot of prior knowledge used in the algorithm, namely we know that the system is a freely rotating rigid body. Therefore, as you emphasise, "learning dynamics" boils down to making good predictions. In figure 3 I observe seemingly good predictions that I can't really conciliate with figure 4, where trajectories clearly differ. Section 6.2 does not provide sufficient explanation for why this is the case.

Because of the freedom os assigning an inertial frame Eq. (4) (and the corresponding Lie-Poisson) and (2) are independent. It is not very clear why you need the latter system of equations? Technically you could perform predictions using only the quaternion equations. Is it only because you want to enforce energy conservation? If so it would be interesting to know how the algorithm performs without this penalty in the loss.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: The paper propose a method to learn dynamics of freely rotating rigid bodies based on the simple idea that in the Hamiltonian framework, rigid bodies dynamics happens on $T^{*}SO(3)$. This makes learned dynamics interpretable and is a concrete step towards more trustworthy, less black-box machine learning models. Despite I asked for a few clarifications, I think the paper is overall clear.
reviews.confidence: 3: You are fairly confident in your assessment. It is possible that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work. Math/other details were not carefully checked.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
The paper propose a method to learn dynamics and make predictions from sequences of images of 3D rigid bodies. The method incorporates physics priors by using SO(3) as the latent space, and by translating the problem of learning the dynamics to that of learning a (reduced) Hamiltonian. Predictions are then made by and integrating the kinematic equations on SO(3), and Lie-Poisson equations on the Lie coalgebra of SO(3).

They perform experiments with a synthetic dataset consisting of sequences of rotating rigid bodies with different shapes and different, diagonal and non-diagonal, inertia tensors.

Strength And Weaknesses:
The methods makes good use of well-known classical mechanics results about freely rotating rigid bodies giving good theoretical reasons for why the method should work. Interpretability of the learned dynamics is of utmost importance when using machine learning to model dynamical systems and the proposed algorithm gives very interpretable results constraining the dynamics on SO(3) and leaving all the unknown and irrelevant characteristics, such as color and shape of the modelled object, to the autoencoder.

Unfortunately, as stated in the paper, there aren't many datasets to assess performances for the particular problem tackled in this paper. 
The dataset could also represent a good benchmark for future works if made available, and therefore is a notable contribution.
The comparison with LSTMs and Neural ODEs is really meaningful as these methods are not as domain-specific as the proposed algorithms but certainly show how inductive biases like the one proposed in this paper are the way to go. Is there any 

Apart from the dynamics, the SO(3) latent space seems to facilitate the reconstruction: perhaps a pre-training of just the encoder-decoder architecture, which is not unrealistic for real-world application, would boost performances. Or perhaps you already do that.

Clarity Quality Novelty And Reproducibility:
The background section is very concise but sufficiently clear for readers that are familiar with Hamiltonian formulation of rigid bodies dynamics.
As claimed by the authors, and to the best of my knowledge it is the first physics-informed deep learning algorithm for predicting image sequences of 3D rigid bodies.

A few questions:

How figure 4 should be interpreted? I struggle to understand why the predicted $S^{2}\times S^{2}$ parameterisation of SO(3) is so different from the ground truth. In this setting there is a lot of prior knowledge used in the algorithm, namely we know that the system is a freely rotating rigid body. Therefore, as you emphasise, "learning dynamics" boils down to making good predictions. In figure 3 I observe seemingly good predictions that I can't really conciliate with figure 4, where trajectories clearly differ. Section 6.2 does not provide sufficient explanation for why this is the case.

Because of the freedom os assigning an inertial frame Eq. (4) (and the corresponding Lie-Poisson) and (2) are independent. It is not very clear why you need the latter system of equations? Technically you could perform predictions using only the quaternion equations. Is it only because you want to enforce energy conservation? If so it would be interesting to know how the algorithm performs without this penalty in the loss.

Summary Of The Review:
The paper propose a method to learn dynamics of freely rotating rigid bodies based on the simple idea that in the Hamiltonian framework, rigid bodies dynamics happens on $T^{*}SO(3)$. This makes learned dynamics interpretable and is a concrete step towards more trustworthy, less black-box machine learning models. Despite I asked for a few clarifications, I think the paper is overall clear.

Rating:
8: accept, good paper
reviews.text:
# Expanded Peer Review

## Summary of the Paper

The paper proposes a method to learn dynamics and make predictions from sequences of images of 3D rigid bodies. The method incorporates physics priors by using SO(3) as the latent space and by translating the problem of learning the dynamics to that of learning a (reduced) Hamiltonian. Predictions are then made by integrating the kinematic equations on SO(3) and Lie-Poisson equations on the Lie coalgebra of SO(3). The approach employs a multi-stage prediction pipeline that maps individual images to a latent representation homeomorphic to SO(3), computes angular velocities from latent pairs, and predicts future latent states using the Hamiltonian equations of motion with a learned representation of the Hamiltonian.

The authors perform experiments with a synthetic dataset consisting of sequences of rotating rigid bodies with different shapes and different inertia tensors—both diagonal (uniform density) and non-diagonal (non-uniform density). This distinction is particularly important as it allows the method to be evaluated on objects with visually identical external geometry but fundamentally different internal mass distributions and thus different rotational dynamics.

## Strengths and Weaknesses

### Strengths

The method makes excellent use of well-known classical mechanics results about freely rotating rigid bodies, providing strong theoretical foundations for why the approach should work. The theoretical framework is particularly well-motivated: by leveraging the Lie-Poisson reduction theorem, the authors elegantly reduce the full dynamics on T*SO(3) to dynamics on the Lie coalgebra so*(3), which is more computationally tractable while maintaining physical correctness. This is a non-trivial theoretical contribution that extends beyond existing work in the literature, which typically focuses on canonical Hamiltonian systems with the canonical symplectic form.

Interpretability of the learned dynamics is of utmost importance when using machine learning to model dynamical systems, and the proposed algorithm gives very interpretable results by constraining the dynamics on SO(3) and leaving all unknown and irrelevant characteristics—such as color and specific shape details—to the autoencoder. This design choice is philosophically sound and practically valuable: it ensures that the learned representation captures only the physically meaningful degrees of freedom while allowing the autoencoder to handle the complexity of rendering and visual appearance. The ability to inspect and understand the latent space as a proper geometric manifold (SO(3)) rather than an opaque vector space is a significant step toward more trustworthy machine learning models, which is increasingly important for safety-critical applications like space robotics mentioned in the introduction.

The comparison with LSTMs and Neural ODEs is genuinely meaningful, as these methods are not as domain-specific as the proposed algorithm but certainly demonstrate how inductive biases like the one proposed in this paper are the way forward. The results in Table 1 show consistent improvements across most datasets, with the proposed method achieving lower test error on the uniform datasets and competitive performance on the non-uniform datasets. Moreover, the proposed approach uses fewer parameters (52,400 for the full model compared to 11,400 for Neural ODE baseline, though the comparison is somewhat complex given different architectures) while maintaining or improving performance, suggesting that the physics inductive bias is genuinely helping the learning process rather than simply adding model capacity.

The dataset itself is a notable contribution. As the authors correctly state, there is a significant deficit of datasets for 3D dynamics learning tasks from images. The creation of four variants—uniform and non-uniform density cubes and prisms—provides a reasonable testbed for future work. The deliberate inclusion of non-uniform density objects is particularly clever, as it tests whether the model can learn dynamics of objects with identical visual appearance but different internal properties, which is directly relevant to the space robotics application domain mentioned in the introduction (detecting internal damage or mass redistribution in satellites).

### Weaknesses

Unfortunately, as stated in the paper, there are not many datasets to assess performance for the particular problem tackled here. While the authors have created a new dataset, it is entirely synthetic and relatively simple, consisting only of 28×28 pixel images of geometric shapes with simple textures. The lack of real-world data or more complex synthetic scenarios (e.g., with variable backgrounds, lighting conditions, occlusions, or multiple objects) is a significant limitation that the authors acknowledge but do not adequately address. The generalization properties of the method to more realistic scenarios remain unclear.

The dataset, while a contribution, could be strengthened considerably. The paper would benefit from more extensive analysis of what the dataset contains and clearer documentation of its structure and availability. While the authors mention that code and dataset links are provided in the appendix, the main paper would benefit from more discussion of dataset statistics, potential failure modes, and limitations. Additionally, the relatively small size of the dataset (1000 trajectories per variant) raises questions about whether the learned models are truly capturing generalizable dynamics or simply memorizing the training data. Cross-validation results or analysis of learning curves would help address this concern.

The paper would also benefit from investigating whether pre-training of the encoder-decoder architecture alone (without the dynamics component) would improve performance. This is not unrealistic for real-world applications where one might have access to large amounts of unlabeled image data before obtaining trajectory data. The ablation study in Appendix C examines the importance of the dynamics and energy losses, but it does not explore the contribution of the autoencoder backbone itself or whether joint training of the encoder-decoder and dynamics is optimal. Understanding the relative contributions of different components would strengthen the experimental analysis.

### Specific Technical Concerns

The experimental evaluation, while showing promise, leaves several questions unanswered. The comparison with HGN (Toth et al., 2020) is particularly puzzling: HGN performs surprisingly poorly on these datasets (Table 1), with test errors significantly worse than the proposed method and even worse than the LSTM baseline on some datasets. This is unexpected given that HGN also uses Hamiltonian priors and was designed for video prediction. The authors should provide more analysis of why HGN underperforms—is this due to architectural differences, hyperparameter choices, or fundamental limitations of the variational approach on these particular datasets?

The energy conservation loss (Equation in Section 4.5) appears to have minimal impact according to the ablation study (Table 4), with removal of the energy loss causing only small degradation in performance. This raises the question of whether energy conservation is truly being leveraged by the model or if it is redundant given the other loss terms. The authors briefly mention that "removing energy loss has a minimal degradation effect on model performance" and cite prior work suggesting this is consistent with the literature, but more investigation into this phenomenon would be valuable.

## Clarity, Quality, Novelty, and Reproducibility

The background section (Section 3) is concise but sufficiently clear for readers familiar with Hamiltonian formulations of rigid body dynamics. The mathematical exposition is generally rigorous and well-presented. The distinction between the canonical Hamiltonian formulation and the Lie-Poisson formulation is clearly explained, and the connection to the reduced dynamics of freely rotating rigid bodies is well-motivated.

As claimed by the authors and to the best of my knowledge, this is the first physics-informed deep learning algorithm for predicting image sequences of 3D rigid bodies using the Hamiltonian formalism. The extension of Hamiltonian neural networks to the Lie-Poisson setting (rather than the canonical setting) is a genuine theoretical contribution that could have broader applicability beyond this specific problem.

The paper is generally well-written and organized. The architecture diagram (Figure 1) effectively communicates the multi-stage pipeline. The implementation details are reasonably complete, with network architectures provided in the appendix and hyperparameters documented for each experiment.

### Questions and Clarifications Needed

Several aspects of the paper warrant further clarification:

**1. Interpretation of Figure 4 and Latent Space Analysis:** How should Figure 4 be interpreted in relation to the visual prediction quality shown in Figure 3? The predicted S²×S² parameterization of SO(3) appears substantially different from the ground truth trajectories in Figure 4, which seems to contradict the seemingly good visual predictions in Figure 3. Section 6.2 claims to provide "latent space analysis and interpretability" but is notably brief and does not provide sufficient explanation for this apparent discrepancy. This is concerning because one of the paper's main contributions is the interpretability of the latent space. A more thorough analysis would include: (1) quantitative metrics comparing predicted and ground-truth trajectories in latent space, (2) explanation of what causes divergence in latent space despite good visual reconstruction, and (3) visualization of multiple trajectories to assess consistency of the learned dynamics.

**2. Independence of Kinematic and Dynamics Equations:** Due to the rotational symmetry of freely rotating rigid bodies (as the authors correctly note), Equation 4 (and the corresponding Lie-Poisson Equation 6) and Equation 2 are independent. It is not immediately clear why both systems of equations are needed in the framework. Technically, one could perform predictions using only the angular momentum equations on so*(3) and then reconstruct the quaternion trajectory afterward. Is the inclusion of the kinematic equations (Equation 2

How figure 4 should be interpreted? I struggle to understand why the predicted $S^{2}\times S^{2}$ parameterisation of SO(3) is so different from the ground truth. In figure 3 I observe seemingly good predictions that I can't really conciliate with figure 4, where trajectories clearly differ. reviews.summary_of_the_paper:
The paper presents a method to estimate 3D rotations of an object from images using classical physics. The paper is focused on predicting  satellite rotations. It uses Hamiltonian representation of rotational motion. With this representation, motion trajectory is differentiable everywhere. This is not true for rotation parametrisation using exponential or quaternion maps as the inverses may be discontinuous. The paper provides a 3D dataset of uniform and non-uniform cube and prism to train the method.
The method uses a set of sequential images to estimate rotation and angular velocities. It encodes images to low-dimensional space representing rotations (learnt during training phase) which are used to estimate angular velocity and predicts next image using Hamiltonian dynamics.
reviews.strength_and_weaknesses:
Strengths:
1. The use of hamiltonian dynamics is novel to the context of rotation estimation and trajectory prediction of satellites.
2. A dataset has been proposed in order to learn 3D dynamics through images.
3. The experiments show convincing results.

Weakness:
1. No evaluation on real data.
2. In figure 4, it seems that the rotation estimation starts well and drifts away quickly. The rotation trajectory prediction is very off while the image prediction is quite decent. Do the images in fugure 3 (upto t=50) represent a small portion of the trajectory show on figure 4. More details are needed.
reviews.clarity_quality_novelty_and_reproducibil: I think that the writing is sufficiently comprehensible except in few sections. The main concepts are made quite clear. The limitations and failure cases have been discussed and the future works have been correctly identified. The authors will release the code upon acceptance to make the method reproducible. The main contribution is novel in the context of satellite trajectory estimation.
reviews.summary_of_the_review:
The paper is definitely interesting. My only concern is the lack of evaluation on real data and the inability of method to deal with non-uniform data. 

After reading the rebuttal and discussion with other reviewers, I have changed my opinion (a bit unfavorably). Here are the main concerns:

1. The experimentation is highly limited. The discussion on handling non-linear mass distribution is non-existent. The authors provide some explanation in the rebuttal but a lot questions remain. Given that the setup is difficult, perhaps the authors could explore non-linearity a bit more. For example, how does the method behave when the mass is non-uniform in a way that the centre of mass deviates strongly with respect to object centre. This discussion could have helped to understand the practical limitations of the method better. Plus, the authors must experiment with more data. Right now, the datasets are limited to a cube and a prism.

2. There were some gaps in writing. The figure 2 in initial draft, which shows the trajectory to drift apart drastically, was later on removed. It is unclear to the readers how the registration works if the estimation of trajectory is so wrong. It is not clear whether the authors are performing a global alignment to fit into the projection. Perhaps the authors should have clarified.

Overall, the paper has an important contribution over sota but the technical novelty is limited. Without a strong experimental evaluation, it was difficult to have an undoubted positive opinion.

# Expanded Review: Learning Interpretable Dynamics from Images of a Freely Rotating 3D Rigid Body

## Summary Of The Paper

The paper presents a physics-informed neural network method for estimating and predicting 3D rotational dynamics of freely rotating rigid bodies from image sequences. The work is motivated by practical applications in space robotics, particularly for resident space objects (RSOs) such as satellites and space debris where onboard cameras provide the primary source of state information. The core methodological contribution lies in combining classical Hamiltonian mechanics with deep learning, specifically leveraging the Lie-Poisson formulation of rigid body dynamics on the SO(3) manifold. The method employs a multi-stage pipeline: (1) encoding individual images to a latent representation homeomorphic to SO(3) via the S²×S² parameterization, (2) estimating angular velocities from sequential latent pairs, (3) predicting future latent states by integrating the learned Hamiltonian equations of motion, and (4) decoding predicted latent states back to image sequences. The authors introduce a new dataset of synthetic rotating rigid bodies with both uniform and non-uniform mass distributions rendered as 28×28 pixel images, and demonstrate that their approach outperforms baseline methods (LSTM, Neural ODE, HGN) on image prediction tasks.

## Strength And Weaknesses

### Strengths

1. **Novel application of Hamiltonian dynamics to rotation estimation**: The use of Hamiltonian representation of rotational motion in the context of learning 3D rigid-body dynamics from images is genuinely novel. The paper correctly identifies that the S²×S² parameterization of SO(3) provides a surjective and differentiable mapping with a continuous right inverse, which is advantageous over exponential maps and quaternion representations that suffer from discontinuous inverses. This is a technically sound observation that addresses a real challenge in deep manifold regression for rotations. The application of the Lie-Poisson reduction theorem to the rigid body problem is mathematically rigorous and extends beyond canonical Hamiltonian formulations typically used in prior work.

2. **Comprehensive dataset creation for 3D dynamics learning**: The authors acknowledge and address a significant gap in the literature by creating the first dataset specifically designed for learning 3D rigid-body dynamics from images. Previous work has focused primarily on 2D planar systems (pendulum, Acrobot, cart-pole, 2-body and 3-body problems). The inclusion of both uniform and non-uniform mass density objects is a thoughtful design choice that attempts to capture the complexity of real-world scenarios where internal mass distribution cannot be directly observed from external geometry.

3. **Convincing experimental results on synthetic data**: The quantitative results in Table 1 demonstrate that the proposed method outperforms three different baseline approaches across most datasets. The model achieves lower pixel mean square error on both training and test sets, often by significant margins. For example, on the uniform cube dataset, the proposed method achieves 3.54±0.17 training MSE compared to 21.55±1.98 for LSTM and 9.43±1.20 for Neural ODE. The qualitative predictions shown in Figure 3 visually demonstrate the model's ability to generate plausible rotational sequences over extended prediction horizons.

4. **Principled approach to interpretability**: The explicit constraint that the latent space be homeomorphic to SO(3) provides genuine interpretability advantages over black-box approaches. This design choice enables direct inspection of learned rotation representations and angular momentum, facilitating error analysis and debugging. The authors correctly emphasize that this interpretability is crucial for deployment in safety-critical applications such as space robotics. The ability to directly access and verify the moment-of-inertia tensor J is a meaningful advantage for understanding what the model has learned about system dynamics.

5. **Thorough mathematical background and technical exposition**: The paper provides clear exposition of the necessary mathematical machinery, including the S²×S² parameterization, 3D rigid-body kinematics and dynamics, and the Lie-Poisson formulation. The background section effectively motivates why classical parametrizations are problematic and why the proposed approach is technically sound. The connection between the canonical Hamiltonian formulation and the generalized Lie-Poisson formulation is well-explained.

### Weaknesses

1. **Complete absence of evaluation on real data**: This is perhaps the most significant limitation of the work. The paper presents no experiments on actual image sequences of real rotating objects, real satellite imagery, or any other real-world data. All results are obtained from synthetic 28×28 pixel renderings of simple geometric objects (cubes and rectangular prisms) in a controlled Blender environment. The gap between synthetic and real data is substantial—real images would involve variable backgrounds, lighting conditions, occlusions, camera noise, and other visual artifacts that are entirely absent from the synthetic setting. The authors acknowledge this limitation briefly in Section 7.2, but the implications are severe: it is fundamentally unclear whether the method would work at all on real satellite imagery or other real rotating objects. For a paper motivated by practical space robotics applications, this is a critical shortcoming.

2. **Severe limitations in handling non-uniform mass distributions**: While the paper claims to address non-uniform density objects, the experimental treatment of this case is superficial and potentially misleading. The non-uniform density datasets are created by simply shifting the mass distribution within the same external geometry, yet the paper provides no analysis of how the method actually learns to distinguish between objects with identical appearances but different internal mass distributions. The claim that "we can learn these very different dynamics even when the external geometry is the same" (Introduction) is not adequately supported by analysis or ablation studies. 

   More critically, the paper does not explore realistic failure modes in non-uniform mass learning. For instance, what happens when the center of mass deviates significantly from the geometric center? How does performance degrade as the mass distribution becomes increasingly complex or asymmetric? The non-uniform cube and prism used in experiments appear to have relatively modest mass asymmetries (as evidenced by the moment-of-inertia tensors in Appendix D.3 and D.4). The paper lacks investigation into whether the method would work for extreme mass distributions, objects with concentrated point masses, or systems where the principal axes of inertia are substantially misaligned with the visual axes of the object. These are precisely the scenarios that would be most challenging and most interesting from a practical standpoint.

3. **Inconsistency and lack of clarity regarding trajectory drift**: The original paper included Figure 2 showing predicted trajectories that "drift away quickly," yet this figure was removed from the final submission without explanation. The review notes that "in figure 4, it seems that the rotation estimation starts well and drifts away quickly. The rotation trajectory prediction is very off while the image prediction is quite decent." This raises serious questions about what is actually being learned. If the predicted rotations diverge from ground truth, how can the decoded images remain visually plausible? This suggests the model may be learning to generate reasonable-looking rotating objects rather than accurately predicting the specific dynamics of the observed object.

   The paper does not adequately address the mechanism by which image-space prediction can remain accurate while rotation-space prediction diverges. Are the encoder and decoder learning to absorb prediction errors through ambiguities in the image-to-rotation mapping? Is the model relying on the smoothness of image space to hide trajectory errors? This fundamental question about what the model is actually learning deserves explicit investigation and explanation. The removal of Figure 2 without discussion compounds this concern, as it appears to hide rather than address the issue.

4. **Limited experimental scope and dataset diversity**: The experimental evaluation is restricted to only four datasets, all of which are variations on two simple geometric shapes (cubes and rectangular prisms). All objects are rendered at the same 28×28 resolution with the same multi-colored texture pattern. There is no exploration of how the method performs with different object geometries, different rendering resolutions, different visual appearances, or different dynamics regimes. 

   The paper would be significantly strengthened by experiments on additional object shapes (spheres, cylinders, irregular polyhedra, etc.), multiple objects of the same shape with different mass distributions, or objects with different visual properties (reflective surfaces, transparent materials, minimal visual features, etc.). The current evaluation only demonstrates that the method works for a narrow class of problems. Broader experimental validation would provide more confidence in the generality of the approach.

5. **Insufficient analysis of the learned Hamiltonian and inertia tensor**: While the paper learns the moment-of-inertia tensor J, there is minimal analysis of whether the learned J values are physically plausible or accurate. The paper does not compare the learned moment-of-inertia tensors to the ground truth values used in the synthetic data generation. For the uniform density objects, the principal moments of inertia should be analytically computable and could serve as ground truth. For non-uniform objects, the paper does not discuss whether the learned J values are consistent with the actual mass distributions. This analysis would provide crucial validation that the model is learning physically meaningful representations rather than simply fitting the data.

6. **Unclear handling of global alignment and registration**: Related to the trajectory drift issue

# Expanded Peer Review: Learning Interpretable Dynamics from Images of a Freely Rotating 3D Rigid Body

## Summary of the Paper

The paper presents a physics-informed neural network model designed to estimate and predict 3D rotational dynamics from image sequences of freely rotating rigid bodies. The approach employs a multi-stage prediction pipeline that accomplishes three key objectives: (1) mapping individual images to a latent representation homeomorphic to SO(3), (2) computing angular velocities from pairs of latent states, and (3) predicting future latent states using the Hamiltonian equations of motion with a learned representation of the Hamiltonian function. The authors validate their approach on a newly created dataset containing sequences of rotating cubes and rectangular prisms with both uniform and non-uniform mass density distributions. The work is motivated by applications to space robotics and resident space object (RSO) dynamics estimation, where onboard cameras provide the only available sensor modality for learning dynamics in proximity operations.

## Strengths

**Strong Theoretical Foundation and Novel Application Domain**

+ The paper presents a well-motivated physics-informed deep learning framework that successfully embeds image observations into the configuration space SO(3) and propagates Hamiltonian dynamics forward in time. This represents a principled approach to incorporating geometric structure into the learning problem. The use of the S²×S² parameterization of SO(3) is particularly well-justified, as it provides a surjective and differentiable mapping with a continuous right inverse—a significant advantage over quaternion or exponential map parameterizations that lack continuous inverses. The mathematical framework is rigorous, with careful treatment of the kinematic equations (Eq. 1-2) and the reduced Hamiltonian formulation via the Lie-Poisson reduction theorem (Eq. 5-6).

+ The generalization from canonical Hamiltonian systems to the Lie-Poisson formulation is a meaningful theoretical contribution. Unlike prior work (Greydanus et al., 2019; Toth et al., 2020) that relies on the canonical symplectic form, this work correctly applies the generalized symplectic form Λ_so*(3) appropriate for systems with rotational symmetry. This extension is non-trivial and opens the door to learning dynamics for a broader class of systems beyond those described by canonical Hamilton's equations.

+ The paper addresses an important and underexplored problem domain. Learning 3D rigid-body dynamics from images is substantially more challenging than the 2D planar systems studied in prior work (Allen-Blanchette et al., 2020; Zhong and Leonard, 2020; Toth et al., 2020). The application motivation—estimating RSO dynamics for space proximity operations—is compelling and practically relevant.

**Dataset Contribution and Experimental Design**

+ The creation of a new rotating rigid-body dataset with 4,000 total trajectories (1,000 per configuration) addresses a significant gap in the literature. As the authors correctly note, existing benchmarks focus on 2D planar dynamics or low-dimensional state spaces, making them unsuitable for evaluating 3D rigid-body rotation learning. The inclusion of non-uniform density objects is particularly valuable, as it demonstrates the model's ability to infer mass distribution properties that are not visually apparent from the external geometry alone—a capability directly relevant to fault detection in space systems where internal mass shifts may occur.

+ The experimental design thoughtfully progresses from uniform density objects (where the principal axes align with intuitive body axes) to non-uniform density objects (where the moment of inertia tensor becomes non-diagonal). This progression effectively isolates the challenges of learning increasingly complex dynamical systems.

**Interpretability and Reduced Parameter Space**

+ The learned latent space is inherently interpretable due to its constraint to SO(3), providing a meaningful low-dimensional representation compared to black-box approaches. This is a significant practical advantage for deployment in safety-critical applications such as space robotics, where understanding and verifying model behavior is essential. The paper correctly emphasizes that this interpretability represents "a step towards producing new frameworks for interpreting deep learning models, analyzing failure modes, and using control for dynamic systems with significant structure from prior knowledge."

+ The model uses fewer parameters (52,400 for the full model) compared to the HGN baseline while achieving superior performance on most datasets. The parameter efficiency, combined with the use of physics-based constraints, suggests a more sample-efficient learning approach.

## Major Weaknesses

**Severely Limited Experimental Validation in Idealized Settings**

- The paper explicitly acknowledges in Section 7.2 that "this approach has only been tested in an idealized setting," yet this limitation is far more severe than the authors' brief treatment suggests. The experimental validation is restricted to synthetic 28×28 pixel images of simple geometric objects (cubes and rectangular prisms) rendered with uniform backgrounds and controlled lighting in Blender. This represents an enormous gap between the experimental setting and the stated application domain of space robotics, where real images contain significant challenges: variable lighting conditions, occluding debris, complex backgrounds, camera noise, motion blur, and the need to operate under realistic sensor constraints.

- The scalability of the approach to realistic image resolutions is completely unexplored. All experiments use extremely low-resolution 28×28 pixel images, which is orders of magnitude smaller than practical camera resolutions on space systems (typically 512×512 or higher). It is unclear whether the encoder-decoder architecture (Tables 2-3) would scale appropriately to higher resolutions, and whether the SO(3) embedding would remain effective with the increased information content and noise present in realistic imagery. The computational cost of the approach at realistic resolutions is not discussed.

- No evaluation is provided on images with variable backgrounds, realistic lighting variations, or partial occlusions. The assumption of clean, centered, well-lit objects in a controlled environment is unrealistic for the stated space robotics applications. The paper would be substantially strengthened by at least one experiment demonstrating robustness to one of these realistic variations, even if preliminary.

- The dataset consists of only 1,000 trajectories per configuration, each of length 100 timesteps. While reasonable for a proof-of-concept, this is relatively modest compared to datasets used in modern computer vision and dynamics learning (e.g., video prediction benchmarks often contain orders of magnitude more data). The generalization properties to unseen object configurations or initial conditions beyond the training distribution are not thoroughly investigated.

**Insufficient Justification of Novelty and Contributions Over Existing Work**

- While the paper claims to be "the first to use the Hamiltonian formalism to learn 3D rigid-body dynamics from images," the novelty over existing work is not sufficiently justified. Duong and Atanasov (2021) already applies Hamiltonian methods to rigid-body dynamics on SO(3) and SE(3), learning the moment of inertia and other parameters from state data. The primary difference is that the current work uses images as input rather than state measurements. However, the image-to-latent mapping is achieved through a standard autoencoder (albeit with an SO(3) constraint), which is relatively straightforward. The core algorithmic contribution—using the Lie-Poisson formulation for dynamics—is a natural extension of Duong and Atanasov's approach rather than a fundamental innovation.

- The comparison to Toth et al. (2020) is acknowledged but not thoroughly analyzed. The paper states that Toth et al. "have a much higher dimension latent space than the physical configuration space," but the implications of this difference are not explored. Does the lower-dimensional latent space actually improve generalization, or does it simply reduce the capacity to capture complex visual phenomena? The ablation studies (Section C) do not address this question.

- The paper does not clearly articulate what aspects of the approach are novel versus what are applications of existing techniques. The S²×S² parameterization is from Falorsi et al. (2018), the quaternion kinematics are standard, the Hamiltonian formulation follows Greydanus et al. (2019) and Duong and Atanasov (2021), and the autoencoder architecture is conventional. The synthesis of these components is competent, but incremental.

**Insufficient Experimental Evaluation and Ablation Studies**

- The quantitative results in Table 1 show that the proposed method outperforms baselines on most datasets, but the improvements are often modest and within the standard deviation ranges reported. For example, on the uniform cube dataset, the test MSE is 3.97±0.16 (ours) vs. 9.48±1.19 (NeuralODE), which is a clear win. However, on the non-uniform prism dataset, the results are 6.61±0.88 (ours) vs. 8.33±0.26 (HGN), and the standard deviations overlap. Statistical significance testing is not provided, making it difficult to assess whether observed differences are meaningful.

- The ablation study (Section C) is incomplete. While it examines the impact of removing the dynamics loss and energy loss, it does not investigate several important design choices:

Summary Of The Paper:
The paper presented a physics-informed neural network model to estimate and predict 3D rotational dynamics from image sequences. The target is achieved by using a multi-stage prediction pipeline that maps individual images to a latent representation homeomorphic to SO(3), computes angular velocities from latent pairs, and predicts future latent states using the Hamiltonian equations of motion with a learned representation of the Hamiltonian.

Strength And Weaknesses:
Strength: 

+ A physics-informed deep learning framework for predicting image sequences of 3D rigid-bodies by embedding the images as measurements in the configuration space SO(3) and propagating the Hamiltonian dynamics forward in time

+ A new rotating rigid-body dataset with sequences of rotating cubes and rectangular prisms with uniform and non-uniform density.

Weaknesses:

- As the paper acknowledged, the paper only evaluated the proposed method in a rather idealized setting. To conclude the current experimental evaluation and ablation studies are insufficient to justify the paper.

- The proposed approach currently relies on the ability to train the model for each system being examined.

- The secod paragraph of the Summary section seems to be overclaiming its contributions without sufficient justification.

Clarity Quality Novelty And Reproducibility:
Clarity: The introduction section should be improved with details of the proposed method. In Fig. 1, what are (a), (b) and (c) actually not clear.

Quality & Novelty: As the paper acknowledged, the paper only evaluated the proposed method in a rather idealized setting. To conclude the current experimental evaluation and ablation studies are insufficient to justify the paper.


Reproducibility: Currently, more details are needed to achieve reproduction of the proposed method.

Summary Of The Review:
The paper presented a physics-informed neural network model to estimate and predict 3D rotational dynamics from image sequences. However, the current experimental evaluation and ablation studies are insufficient to justify the paper. The novelty/contribution of the proposed method over existing work has not been fully justified.

reviews.strength_and_weaknesses:
Strengths

This research is impressive because it is a challenging task to realize a neural network method that learns a physical model capable of predicting video images. The fact that they have succeeded in predicting and reconstructing video images to some extent by incorporating a physical inductive bias specific to rotational motion into the model is also highly novel.



Weaknesses

The following two points are considered weaknesses


The advantage over previous research is unclear:

Hamiltonian Generative Networks (HGN) [1] has been shown to be able to learn up to 400 steps in the future for a two-body problem [2].
HGN is also more general-purpose than the proposed method.
The authors need to show that the proposed method has better prediction performance for videos than HGN.

[1] Peter Toth, Danilo J. Rezende, Andrew Jaegle, Sébastien Racanière, Aleksandar Botev, Irina Higgins, "Hamiltonian Generative Networks," ICLR 2020, 2019.

[2] Irina Higgins, Peter Wirnsberger, Andrew Jaegle, Aleksandar Botev, "SyMetric: Measuring the Quality of Learnt Hamiltonian Dynamics Inferred from Vision," NeurIPS 2021, 2021.



The purpose of the method is unclear:.

The author writes that the application of the study is to estimate the orientation of satellites, but the training data in this study assigns a different color to each side of the object. I think it does not match the configuration of the satellites. If the differences between each side of a satellite can be determined, the orientation and motion of a satellite with a known geometry can be estimated analytically. Therefore, the proposed method is not considered necessary to achieve the objectives of the study.

Also, the work [Duong and Atanasov (2021)] cited by the authors in section "2.3 LEARNING DYNAMICS FOR RIGID BODIES" to achieve more general learning of rigid body motions by estimating inverse mass properties could be used for satellite orientation estimation, but no comparative experiments have been conducted. 

These factors made me think that the relationship between the proposed method and the purpose of the study did not seem appropriate.

reviews.text:
# Expanded Peer Review: Learning Interpretable Dynamics from Images of a Freely Rotating 3D Rigid Body

## Summary of the Paper

The manuscript proposes a method for estimating and predicting 3D rotation dynamics from image sequences of freely rotating 3D rigid bodies such as satellites. The proposed method maps individual images to latent representations isomorphic to SO(3) using auto-encoder-type neural networks, calculates angular velocities from latent pairs, and then learns the moment of inertia tensor J within a Hamiltonian framework. The approach leverages the Lie-Poisson reduction theorem to formulate dynamics on the reduced Hamiltonian space, which is a theoretically elegant contribution. Based on this prediction of future latent states, a predictive image is constructed using the auto-encoder decoder. After constructing a new rotating rigid body dataset consisting of sequences of rotating cubes and rectangular prisms with both uniform and non-uniform mass distributions, the authors show that the proposed method gives higher performance than LSTM and NeuralODE baselines on their synthetic datasets.

## Strengths

### Novel Application of Lie-Poisson Formalism

This research is impressive because it addresses a challenging task: realizing a neural network method that learns a physical model capable of predicting video sequences. The fact that the authors have succeeded in predicting and reconstructing video images to a reasonable extent by incorporating a physics-informed inductive bias specific to 3D rotational motion into the model is highly novel. The use of the Lie-Poisson reduction theorem to derive the generalized symplectic form as a physics prior is a particularly elegant theoretical contribution that extends beyond existing canonical Hamiltonian approaches used in prior work (Greydanus et al., 2019; Toth et al., 2020). This theoretical framework naturally handles the rotational symmetry of freely rotating rigid bodies and provides a principled way to learn the reduced Hamiltonian on so*(3) rather than the full canonical phase space.

### Interpretable Latent Space

The constraint that the latent space be homeomorphic to SO(3) is a significant advantage over black-box approaches. As the authors note, this provides interpretability that is essential for safety-critical applications such as space proximity operations. The use of the S² × S² parameterization, which possesses a continuous right inverse (unlike quaternions or exponential maps), is a well-motivated technical choice that facilitates deep manifold regression. This interpretability stands in contrast to the much higher-dimensional latent spaces used in more general-purpose approaches like HGN, where the physical meaning of latent dimensions is obscured.

### Handling of Non-uniform Mass Distributions

The work demonstrates an interesting capability: predicting dynamics for objects with non-uniform mass distributions where the principal axes of rotation do not align with the visible body axes. This is a meaningful contribution, as the authors correctly note that mass distribution cannot be easily inferred from external geometry alone. The ability to learn different moment-of-inertia tensors for objects with identical visual appearance but different internal mass distributions (as shown in their uniform vs. non-uniform datasets) is non-trivial and relevant to real applications.

### Dataset Contribution

The creation of new synthetic datasets for 3D rigid-body rotation learning addresses a genuine gap in the literature. Previous work (Greydanus et al., 2019; Toth et al., 2020; Allen-Blanchette et al., 2020; Zhong and Leonard, 2020) has primarily focused on 2D planar systems. The authors' datasets with both uniform and non-uniform density objects provide a useful benchmark for future research in this area.

## Major Weaknesses

### 1. Unclear Advantage over Previous Research

The critical weakness of this work is the failure to convincingly demonstrate superiority over existing methods, particularly Hamiltonian Generative Networks (HGN). While the authors claim novelty in applying the Hamiltonian formalism to 3D rigid-body dynamics from images, the practical advantages remain unclear.

**Concerning Performance Comparison:** Table 1 presents quantitative results comparing the proposed method to three baselines: LSTM, NeuralODE, and HGN. However, the comparison is problematic in several respects:

- The proposed method outperforms the baselines on the synthetic datasets, but these results are measured on relatively short prediction horizons (30 steps with Δt = 10⁻³, corresponding to 0.03 seconds). The authors do not report long-term prediction performance (e.g., 100+ steps), which would be more convincing and more relevant to practical applications like satellite tracking.

- HGN (Toth et al., 2020) has been demonstrated in the literature to successfully predict 400+ steps into the future for two-body problems (as shown in Higgins et al., 2021 "SyMetric: Measuring the Quality of Learnt Hamiltonian Dynamics Inferred from Vision"). The current paper does not attempt such long-horizon predictions, making it difficult to assess whether the proposed method's theoretical advantages translate to practical improvements.

- The HGN baseline results in Table 1 appear surprisingly poor compared to published results. The test MSE for HGN on the uniform cube dataset (18.69 × 10⁻³) is substantially worse than the proposed method (3.97 × 10⁻³), but without detailed analysis of why HGN underperforms on this specific problem, it is unclear whether this reflects a fundamental limitation of the approach or an implementation/hyperparameter issue.

**Missing Ablations and Analysis:** The paper does not provide sufficient analysis of why the proposed method should outperform HGN beyond the theoretical elegance of using the reduced Hamiltonian formulation. Both methods incorporate Hamiltonian structure as an inductive bias. The key differences—the use of SO(3) latent space versus higher-dimensional latent space, the use of Lie-Poisson reduction versus canonical Hamiltonian form—are not empirically isolated or analyzed. An ablation study comparing the proposed method with a variant using canonical Hamiltonian form on the same latent space would help clarify the contribution of the Lie-Poisson reduction.

**Generality vs. Specificity Trade-off:** The authors acknowledge that HGN is "more general-purpose" than their method, yet they do not discuss what is sacrificed by this specialization to SO(3). The proposed method is specifically designed for freely rotating 3D rigid bodies, while HGN is designed for general Hamiltonian systems. It is unclear whether the performance improvements (where they exist) justify this reduced generality, especially given that the improvements are modest and achieved on synthetic data.

### 2. Questionable Alignment Between Method and Stated Application

The stated motivation for this work is learning dynamics of resident space objects (RSOs) and satellites for proximity operations and debris removal. However, the experimental validation does not reflect realistic satellite scenarios, raising fundamental questions about the practical relevance of the approach.

**Unrealistic Experimental Setup:** The authors explicitly state: "The author writes that the application of the study is to estimate the orientation of satellites, but the training data in this study assigns a different color to each side of the object. I think it does not match the configuration of the satellites." This is a critical observation. Real satellites typically have:
- Complex, non-uniform visual appearance with solar panels, antennae, and structural elements
- Variable lighting conditions and surface reflectance properties
- Possible occlusions and partial visibility
- No convenient color coding on each face

In contrast, the experimental datasets consist of simple geometric shapes (cubes and rectangular prisms) with distinct colors on each face and uniform lighting. This is a highly idealized scenario that bears little resemblance to real satellite imagery.

**Implicit Assumption of Known Geometry:** The authors note that "if the differences between each side of a satellite can be determined, the orientation and motion of a satellite with a known geometry can be estimated analytically." The experimental setup essentially assumes this condition—the color-coded faces make orientation trivially identifiable from the images. This undermines the motivation for using deep learning at all. In real scenarios where satellite geometry is known but surface appearance is complex, traditional computer vision methods (e.g., model-based pose estimation) may be more appropriate than learning-based approaches.

**Missing Comparison to Relevant Baselines:** The paper compares against LSTM, NeuralODE, and HGN, but does not compare against the most relevant prior work for their stated application: Duong and Atanasov (2021). The authors cite this work in Section 2.3, noting that it "investigates learning rigid-body dynamics from state-control trajectory data using a Hamiltonian approach" and "focus on systems with configuration spaces on the special orthogonal groups and special Euclidean groups (e.g. SO(2), SO(3), and SE(3))." Duong and Atanasov (2021) addresses the same problem class (learning rigid-body dynamics with unknown inertia properties) and is explicitly designed for SO(3) systems. The authors dismiss this work with the statement "In contrast, our work

title: Unveiling Transformers with LEGO: A Synthetic Reasoning Task

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: The paper proposes a synthetic task that aims to capture aspects of multi-hop reasoning, and uses it to characterize the behavior of Transformer models. It finds that pre-trained transformers learn the task more rapidly, and attributes this to specific patterns of attention in the network. Introducing mechanisms that emulate these specific attention patterns helps close this gap in performance without the need for expensive pre-training. Models struggle with extrapolating to longer chains of reasoning than required during training. This deterioration can be mitigated by repeating layers with the same parameters as well as training with stochastic depth.
reviews.strength_and_weaknesses:
Strengths:
- The proposed task is useful as a tool for understanding some ways in which a Transformer can perform multi-hop reasoning
- The proposed operationalization of requiring length extrapolation is effective at disentangling issues with positional encoding from the question of whether the model can extrapolate to longer chains of reasoning 
- Experiments help provide an understanding for how pre-training, as well as stochastic depth, help improve certain aspects of the models' capabilities
- Hardcoded attention patterns are shown to be just as effective for solving this specific task
- The writing of the paper is clear and well-structured

Weaknesses:
- One of the strengths of the attention mechanism is that it can learn any attention pattern a task may require, and the multi-head version can learn when (at what layer) and how much to use each pattern. The hardcoded attention patterns seem sufficient for the proposed synthetic task -- almost by construction of the LEGO task. There are some preliminary findings that they may also be effective for masked language modeling and at least SQuAD, but this direction is underexplored and perhaps not all of the necessary components for real NLP tasks are captured.
- The proposed task is perhaps not the best test-bed of *pre-training* specifically, because the approach used is to pre-train on natural language but then test on synthetic expressions. It would not be surprising for language-specific reasoning to not undergo transfer in this case, potentially leaving only the most general of structural patterns, like the attention patterns revealed in this work.
- Overall, it's not clear how much of the Transformers' capabilities are stressed by the proposed synthetic task. It could be that understanding multi-hop computation in the style of the LEGO task is the key for explaining current behaviors and proposing improvements. Alternatively, multi-hop reasoning may just not be a crucial component of many NLP tasks as currently formulated, or LEGO might not be capturing the actual character of chain-based reasoning as required for real-world task.
reviews.clarity_quality_novelty_and_reproducibil:
See above

Minor:
- Regarding Transformers that repeat layers: in addition to ALBERT, it would be helpful to cite other instances such as Universal Transformers (Dehghani et al)
reviews.summary_of_the_review: My overall recommendation is based on weighing the thorough and well-written exposition/investigation of the LEGO task with the potential downsides of any synthetic benchmark, namely that it may or may not capture the key elements of actual real-world tasks, and can therefore be limited in its impact.
reviews.confidence: 3: You are fairly confident in your assessment. It is possible that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work. Math/other details were not carefully checked.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
The paper proposes a synthetic task that aims to capture aspects of multi-hop reasoning, and uses it to characterize the behavior of Transformer models. It finds that pre-trained transformers learn the task more rapidly, and attributes this to specific patterns of attention in the network. Introducing mechanisms that emulate these specific attention patterns helps close this gap in performance without the need for expensive pre-training. Models struggle with extrapolating to longer chains of reasoning than required during training. This deterioration can be mitigated by repeating layers with the same parameters as well as training with stochastic depth.

- The proposed task is useful as a tool for understanding some ways in which a Transformer can perform multi-hop reasoning

- Experiments help provide an understanding for how pre-training, as well as stochastic depth, help improve certain aspects of the models' capabilities
- Hardcoded attention patterns are shown to be just as effective for solving this specific task
- The writing of the paper is clear and well-structured

Weaknesses:

The hardcoded attention patterns seem sufficient for the proposed synthetic task -- almost by construction of the LEGO task. - The proposed task is perhaps not the best test-bed of *pre-training* specifically, because the approach used is to pre-train on natural language but then test on synthetic expressions. It would not be surprising for language-specific reasoning to not undergo transfer in this case, potentially leaving only the most general of structural patterns, like the attention patterns revealed in this work.
- Overall, it's not clear how much of the Transformers' capabilities are stressed by the proposed synthetic task. It could be that understanding multi-hop computation in the style of the LEGO task is the key for explaining current behaviors and proposing improvements. Alternatively, multi-hop reasoning may just not be a crucial component of many NLP tasks as currently formulated, or LEGO might not be capturing the actual character of chain-based reasoning as required for real-world task.

Clarity Quality Novelty And Reproducibility:
See above

Minor:
- Regarding Transformers that repeat layers: in addition to ALBERT, it would be helpful to cite other instances such as Universal Transformers (Dehghani et al)

Summary Of The Review:
My overall recommendation is based on weighing the thorough and well-written exposition/investigation of the LEGO task with the potential downsides of any synthetic benchmark, namely that it may or may not capture the key elements of actual real-world tasks, and can therefore be limited in its impact.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
# Expanded Peer Review

## Summary Of The Paper

The paper proposes LEGO (Learning Equality and Group Operations), a synthetic task designed to capture aspects of multi-hop reasoning and chain-of-thought computation. The authors use this task to systematically characterize the behavior of Transformer models, investigating how architectural choices and pretraining affect their ability to perform reasoning. The work finds that pre-trained transformers learn the task more rapidly than randomly initialized models, and attributes this advantage to specific patterns of attention that emerge during pretraining. The authors propose mechanisms that emulate these specific attention patterns—namely association (global matching of identical tokens) and manipulation (local short-range operations)—which help close the performance gap without requiring expensive pretraining. A key contribution is the observation that models struggle significantly with extrapolating to longer chains of reasoning than required during training, a limitation that can be partially mitigated through architectural choices like weight-tied layers (ALBERT) and training with stochastic depth.

## Strengths

- **Well-motivated synthetic task**: The proposed LEGO task is useful and intuitive as a tool for understanding how Transformers perform multi-hop reasoning. The task cleanly separates the problem into two fundamental components—association (long-range identity matching between variable occurrences) and manipulation (short-range group operations)—which provides a clear lens for analyzing model behavior. The formalization using finite groups acting on sets provides mathematical rigor while remaining interpretable.

- **Effective operationalization of length extrapolation**: The paper's key innovation of requiring length extrapolation (training on supervision for the first n_tr variables but testing on all n variables) is particularly effective at disentangling issues with positional encoding from the question of whether the model can truly generalize to longer chains of reasoning. This is an important methodological contribution, as it avoids confounding factors that plague traditional length generalization benchmarks. The distinction between classical generalization (same distribution at train and test time) and length extrapolation (distribution shift in sequence length) is well-articulated and operationally useful.

- **Thorough empirical investigation with clear insights**: The experiments systematically investigate multiple dimensions of transformer learning, including architectural variants (BERT vs. ALBERT), initialization schemes (random vs. pretrained), and data composition effects. The paper effectively uses LEGO as a controlled setting to isolate specific factors. The information percolation analysis in Figure 4, which visualizes how information about variable values emerges at different layers, provides compelling evidence that ALBERT implements something closer to the "natural" for-loop solution compared to BERT's more distributed approach.

- **Novel identification of structured attention patterns**: The paper makes a valuable contribution by identifying and characterizing the association pattern—a global attention mechanism that attends only to identical tokens across the entire sequence. To the best of the reviewers' knowledge, this specific pattern has not been previously highlighted in the literature on transformer attention analysis, despite its apparent importance for reasoning tasks. The visualization in Figure 7 clearly demonstrates these patterns in pretrained BERT without any finetuning.

- **Hypothesis-driven verification methodology**: The mimicking experiments in Section 4.2.2 are particularly clever and well-designed. By training models to match target attention patterns on random data (without access to pretraining), the authors provide causal evidence that the advantage of pretrained BERT stems largely from having learned these structured attention patterns, rather than from semantic understanding of tokens or other factors. The fact that mimicking closes much of the gap between randomly initialized and pretrained BERT is striking and suggests a concrete mechanism for pretraining's effectiveness.

- **Clear and well-structured presentation**: The paper is exceptionally well-written with clear motivation, logical flow, and effective visualizations. The progression from task definition to architectural analysis to proposed improvements is easy to follow. The use of concrete examples (e.g., the sentence "a = +1; b = −a; e = +b; d = −f; c = +d; f = +e") helps ground abstract concepts.

- **Practical architectural contribution**: The hardcoded attention mechanism represents a concrete contribution that could have practical impact. Achieving comparable or better performance on BERT pretraining while reducing FLOPs (Figure 9) and model size is noteworthy, even if the evaluation on downstream tasks is preliminary.

## Weaknesses

- **Limited scope and generalizability of hardcoded patterns**: One of the fundamental strengths of the attention mechanism is its flexibility—it can learn any attention pattern a task may require, and the multi-head version can dynamically determine when and how much to use each pattern across layers. The hardcoded attention patterns appear sufficient for the proposed synthetic task, but this is almost by construction, given that LEGO was specifically designed to require association and manipulation operations. While the authors provide preliminary findings that hardcoded patterns may also be effective for masked language modeling and SQuAD (Section 5), this direction is significantly underexplored. The paper does not provide sufficient evidence that the attention patterns identified in LEGO capture all (or even most) of the necessary components for real NLP tasks. Real language understanding likely requires attention patterns beyond those identified here—patterns for syntax, semantics, discourse structure, and other linguistic phenomena that do not appear in the LEGO task. The authors acknowledge this limitation in Section 6 by noting "There are many important attention heads beyond just manipulation and association, and their roles remain to be discovered," but this acknowledgment somewhat undermines the generality of the proposed architectural changes. The evaluation on downstream tasks (only BERT pretraining and SQuAD) is minimal, and it remains unclear whether the Hybrid model would maintain its advantages on a broader range of NLP tasks or if performance would degrade when models encounter linguistic phenomena not present in LEGO.

- **Questionable transferability of pretraining insights**: The paper's treatment of pretraining is somewhat limited by an important confound: the approach trains on natural language but tests on synthetic expressions. It would not be surprising for language-specific reasoning patterns learned during pretraining to fail to transfer to this artificial setting, potentially leaving only the most general and structural patterns—exactly the attention patterns revealed in this work. This raises the question of whether the paper's conclusions about pretraining are actually informative about real pretraining benefits in practice. When pretrained BERT is applied to actual NLP tasks, it may be leveraging much richer linguistic knowledge than what appears in the LEGO setting. The fact that replacing tokens with arbitrary symbols does not affect pretrained BERT's performance (mentioned briefly in Section 4.2.1) is interesting but not entirely conclusive, as the model may still be using other structural properties of the pretrained representations. The paper would be strengthened by a more nuanced discussion of what aspects of pretraining transfer to synthetic tasks versus what aspects are task-specific. Additionally, the mimicking procedure, while clever, only partially closes the gap between random initialization and pretraining (Figure 6), suggesting that other factors beyond the identified attention patterns also contribute to pretraining's benefits—factors that remain unexplored.

- **Unclear practical significance of the synthetic task**: While LEGO is well-designed as a research tool, it remains unclear how much of Transformers' capabilities in real-world NLP are actually stressed by this task. The paper does not make a convincing case that multi-hop reasoning in the style of LEGO is a key bottleneck for current NLP systems or that understanding LEGO is essential for improving real-world performance. Multi-hop reasoning may simply not be a crucial component of many NLP tasks as currently formulated, or LEGO might not be capturing the actual character of chain-based reasoning as required for real-world applications. For instance, in natural language, reasoning chains are often implicit, ambiguous, and intertwined with linguistic phenomena like coreference resolution, which differ significantly from the explicit, deterministic chains in LEGO. The paper would benefit from empirical evidence demonstrating that insights from LEGO transfer to improvements on standard NLP benchmarks or that failures on LEGO correlate with failures on real reasoning tasks. The fact that the Hybrid model shows only marginal improvements on SQuAD (88.1 vs 88.2 F1 for BERT) suggests that the architectural insights from LEGO may have limited impact on practical NLP performance.

- **Limited exploration of the relationship between task structure and architectural requirements**: While the paper effectively demonstrates that ALBERT outperforms BERT on length extrapolation (Figures 3 and 4), the analysis of why this occurs could be deeper. The paper argues that ALBERT's weight-tied structure provides an inductive bias toward learning a for-loop, but the connection between this architectural property and the specific structure of LEGO could be explored more rigorously. For instance, does ALBERT's advantage depend on the linear chain structure of LEGO, or would it generalize to other reasoning tasks with different computational structures? The paper briefly mentions Task 2 (using the non-abelian permutation group on 3 elements) and defers most analysis to the appendix, but a more thorough investigation of how task structure affects architectural requirements would strengthen the claims about the general utility of iterative architectures for reasoning.

- **Incomplete analysis of shortcut solutions**: Section 4.3 and Appendix A discuss how models learn "

Summary Of The Paper:
The paper proposes a synthetic task that aims to capture aspects of multi-hop reasoning, and uses it to characterize the behavior of Transformer models. It finds that pre-trained transformers learn the task more rapidly, and attributes this to specific patterns of attention in the network. Introducing mechanisms that emulate these specific attention patterns helps close this gap in performance without the need for expensive pre-training. Models struggle with extrapolating to longer chains of reasoning than required during training. This deterioration can be mitigated by repeating layers with the same parameters as well as training with stochastic depth.

Strength And Weaknesses:
Strengths:
- The proposed task is useful as a tool for understanding some ways in which a Transformer can perform multi-hop reasoning
- The proposed operationalization of requiring length extrapolation is effective at disentangling issues with positional encoding from the question of whether the model can extrapolate to longer chains of reasoning 
- Experiments help provide an understanding for how pre-training, as well as stochastic depth, help improve certain aspects of the models' capabilities
- Hardcoded attention patterns are shown to be just as effective for solving this specific task
- The writing of the paper is clear and well-structured

Weaknesses:
- One of the strengths of the attention mechanism is that it can learn any attention pattern a task may require, and the multi-head version can learn when (at what layer) and how much to use each pattern. The hardcoded attention patterns seem sufficient for the proposed synthetic task -- almost by construction of the LEGO task. There are some preliminary findings that they may also be effective for masked language modeling and at least SQuAD, but this direction is underexplored and perhaps not all of the necessary components for real NLP tasks are captured.
- The proposed task is perhaps not the best test-bed of *pre-training* specifically, because the approach used is to pre-train on natural language but then test on synthetic expressions. It would not be surprising for language-specific reasoning to not undergo transfer in this case, potentially leaving only the most general of structural patterns, like the attention patterns revealed in this work.
- Overall, it's not clear how much of the Transformers' capabilities are stressed by the proposed synthetic task. It could be that understanding multi-hop computation in the style of the LEGO task is the key for explaining current behaviors and proposing improvements. Alternatively, multi-hop reasoning may just not be a crucial component of many NLP tasks as currently formulated, or LEGO might not be capturing the actual character of chain-based reasoning as required for real-world task.

Clarity Quality Novelty And Reproducibility:
See above

Minor:
- Regarding Transformers that repeat layers: in addition to ALBERT, it would be helpful to cite other instances such as Universal Transformers (Dehghani et al)

Summary Of The Review:
My overall recommendation is based on weighing the thorough and well-written exposition/investigation of the LEGO task with the potential downsides of any synthetic benchmark, namely that it may or may not capture the key elements of actual real-world tasks, and can therefore be limited in its impact.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary_of_the_paper:
The paper provides empirical investigation of transformer by constructing a synthetic reasoning task -- LEGO (Learning Equality and Group Operations). The LEGO tasks consist of group operations and a chain of reasoning. The major purpose of the synthetic experiment is to provide a controlled setting and unveil what transformer learns. To do so, the paper conduct a series of experiments on the both classic setting (training and testing distribution are the same) and the OOD setting (out of distribution) and they found

(1) Classical generalization happens all the architecture.

(2) OOD generalization depends on the architecture and data preparation.

(3) The non-extrapolating models (architecture fails for OOD) learns short-cut solution.

The author also propose hardcode attention layer that incorporates knowledge over the dataset that seems to work well on LEGO.

Strength. The paper provides solid contribution towards understanding the Transformer architecture. By constructing a synthetic benchmark, it provides a series experiment that provide new insight and confirm existing observations. 

Weakness. There is no major weakness.

The related work section can be improved, in particular, there is a previous line of work "unveils" transformer over regular language ( in particular dyck language) that should be cited and discussed. Examples include [1] (and see the reference therein), which studies the dyck language and provide some theory and empirical investigation on what transformer learns.

[1] Self-Attention Networks Can Process Bounded Hierarchical Languages. Shunyu Yao, Binghui Peng, Christos Papadimitriou, Karthik Narasimhan. ACL'2021

The paper provides solid contribution to the literature and I vote for acceptance.


Some minor questions over "shortcut":
The paper finds transformer sometime learns short-cut solution to the reasoning tasks. However, it is unclear to me why this is a bad thing. I feel it is not equivalent to spurious feature because some reasoning tasks involves short-cut solution or parallelizable solution, e.g., it is possible that a linear size circuit only has depth $O(\log n)$ (i.e., do not follow the chain of reasoning). Some further discussions should be a good addition on the finding!
